Suervisor: Dr Eddie Mwebesa

p.bold: A RETROSPECTIVE ASSESSMENT OF THE SPIRITUAL NEEDS AND THEIR PREVALENCE AMONGST PALLIATIVE CARE PATIENTS AT HOSPICE AFRICA UGANDA

body.p: Renner C1, Natuhwera G2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Hospice Africa Uganda, Kampala, Uganda

There is increasing research supporting the importance of spiritual care within palliative care (PC). However, this research is sparse in low-income countries (LICs). This study sought to document the prevalence of spiritual needs among cancer patients receiving PC at Hospice Africa Uganda (HAU).

107 randomly sampled case notes of cancer patients were reviewed. Data was collected using a tri-categorical questionnaire based on HAU and the HOPE spiritual needs assessment tools. Data was entered, coded and analyzed in excel. Statistical analysis performed included single factor ANOVA and two-sample T-tests, using a 5% significance level. P-values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Data were organized into three categories-: organized religion (OR), personal spirituality (PS), and personal implications (PI). Majority (70%) were female. Regarding diagnosis, 64.5% had cancer, 18.7% had cancer and HIV/AIDS, and 12.2% had cancer and other comorbidities. 99% of the patients considered religion important, 97% prayed and 98% found prayer helpful. Spiritual practices had positive effects-: better sleep, maintaining hope, peace and comfort, and practical support. 13.6% of patients reported changing religion, usually in a search for healing. Inability to receive sacraments and congregate for prayers were identified as common causes of spiritual distress. The mean comparison tests point to an association between the patient’s gender and presenting with unrelieved pain, p-value (0.044), α (0.05). Frequency data indicated that males may be at a higher risk of experiencing unrelieved pain than females (87% versus 68%).

This study demonstrated the importance of maintaining and facilitating fulfillment of spiritual needs within holistic PC, and also the pressing need to address unrelieved pain and psychological problems which are potential catalysts for spiritual distress.
Persenting Author:Carl Renner
Supervisor: Dr Germans Natuhwera

p.bold: A RETROSPECTIVE STUDY ON CHANGING PATTERNS OF CANCER DIAGNOSIS AMONG ADULT FEMALES AT HOSPICE AFRICA UGANDA, MBARARA

body.p: Goubkine D1, Birungi AD2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Clinical Department, Hospice Africa Uganda, Makindye Division, Kampala, Uganda

Hospice Africa Uganda (HAU) is a palliative care organisation which provides essential cancer palliation to the community in which it resides. Currently, the burden of female cancer is a grand unmet challenge in African healthcare where female patients receive marginalized care and face worse outcomes from infection-related cancers. 1﻿ ​2 Cancer research focused primarily on females results in data which allows for provisional targeted care. The aim of this review, spanning across 25 years, was to identify cancer prevalence and report on trends among HAU’s female patients at the Mbarara site.

This study was a retrospective chart inquiry, sampling all records of female patients over the age of 18 with a positive cancer diagnosis (n=13,646) from 1995 to 2020. Data was analyzed for each five-year interval and then cross referenced to represent the progression. Data showed that over the 25-year period there were six cancer types that remained among the top five most diagnosed cancers among hospice patients (n=9615): Cervical, breast, Kaposi sarcoma, upper gastrointestinal (esophageal and stomach), lower gastrointestinal (colon, rectal, anal) and hepatocellular carcinoma. Cervical cancer prevailed as the most common cancer making up an average of 43% of the overall prevalence of the top 6 cancers between 1995 and 2020. While breast cancer (21%) followed a close second. Together cervical and breast cancer made up an average 64.3% of the top 6 cancers over the time frame. The trends described in this study provide a brief outlook into where female cancer will go in the future for Hospice Africa Uganda. This study has important implications for the building of a more targeted health service infrastructure of cancer management in the region. Further studies are imperative for detailing the pattern between infection and female cancers and whether the next decade will see the decline of vaccine-mediated cancers in the area.
This project was supported financially by University College Dublin School of Medicine.

list-item.p: Arbyn, Marc et al. Estimates of incidence and mortality of cervical cancer in 2018: a worldwide analysis. The Lancet Global Health, Volume 8, Issue 2, e191 - e203
Obol, J.H., Lin, S., Obwolo, M.J. et al. Knowledge, attitudes, and practice of cervical cancer prevention among health workers in rural health centres of Northern Uganda. BMC Cancer 21, 110 (2021).

body.p: Presenting Author: Dasha Goubkine
Supervisor: Dr Agasha D. Birungi

p.bold: THE SURVIVAL OF CERVICAL CANCER PATIENTS AT HOSPICE AFRICA UGANDA

body.p: Fogarty J1, Adong Olet D2, Nalubega H2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Hospice Africa Uganda, Kampala, Uganda
The objective of this project is to describe the survival of cervical cancer patients at Hospice Africa Uganda (HAU).

A retrospective study of the case notes of patients with a diagnosis of cervical cancer was carried out. 75 case notes were selected using purposive sampling, reduced to a sample size of 63 by removing data which didn’t fit the inclusion criteria. The case notes were from patients with confirmed cervical cancer diagnoses who presented/were admitted to HAU from 1993 to 2022. HAU introduced the Road to Care Programme in 2012, endeavouring to improve access to cancer treatments including radiotherapy. This study aims to investigate the impact of the Road to Care programme by comparing survival of patients from before and after 2012 in a descriptive analysis.

There is a correlation between survival and the stage of cervical cancer of the patient at admission to HAU. All patients in the study who had died at last contact with HAU had either stage 3 or 4 cervical cancer. 31 patients had stage 3B, 4A or 4B cervical cancer, and 6 died (19.35%, n=31). All of these patients were admitted to HAU before 2012. Of all patients admitted to HAU before 2012, 7 died (24.14%, n=34). Of all patients admitted to HAU after 2012, 1 died (3.45%, n=29).
These results indicate an increase in survival among patients at HAU in the period after 2012, suggesting that the Road to Care Programme was beneficial.
Results of survival estimates are to follow and will be calculated using Kaplan-Meier curves.
The author would like to acknowledge an SSRA Dean’s Scholarship from the Medicine International Office.
Presenting Author: Jennifer Fogarty
Supervisor: Dr Dorothy Olet Adong
Co-Supervisor: Ms Harriet Nalubega

p.bold: EFFECTS OF TIMING OF CALORIC INTAKE ON WEIGHT LOSS

body.p: Chen K1, Bai D2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Lifestyle LLC, Seattle, Washington, USA

Weight control is a prominent recommendation in many aspects of healthcare. Timing of caloric intake represents a target to help control weight. The aim of this research is to (a) evaluate the evidence supporting when a majority of calories should be consumed and (b) identify considerations for new research opportunities to maximize weight loss.

This study was designed as a non-systematic literature review. Various key words/terms were used as inclusion criteria such as “meal” “timing” “ghrelin” “diet” “circadian rhythm”. The main exclusion criteria was avoidance of an overemphasis on the duration of fasting.

All recent studies have shown to agree that consuming more calories during “breakfast” is shown to be associated with the largest reduction in BMI in isocaloric diets. Various explanations for the weight loss have been proposed such as energy efficiency such as heat generation; metabolic processes such as glucose tolerance; and reduced hunger levels. Further studies have shown that irregular eating schedules can promote weight gain.

The projected results of the ongoing study support popular trends to strive to consume a larger portion of calories earlier; however, the broad usage of the terms breakfast, lunch, and dinner are identified as a limiting factor in studies. Future studies should be pursued and monitored in relationship to both absolute timing as well as relative timing in relationship to when individuals wake up. Additional studies are needed to be designed to understand an individual success chance and risk while making adjustments to shift to earlier calorie consumption.
Presenting Author:Kevin Chen
Supervisor:Dr Diane Bai

p.bold: TRANSFORMING INTRAOPERATIVE BREAST CANCER POST-MASTECTOMY INDOCYANINE GREEN ANGIOGRAMS INTO RELEVANT INPUT FOR PREDICTIVE MODELS

body.p: Jindal A1, 2, Dalli J1, 2, Nguyen CL3, 4, 5, Epperlein JP6, Warrier S3, 4, 5, Cahill RA1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Centre for Precision Surgery, School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
3Department of Breast Surgery, Chris O’Brien Lifehouse, Camperdown, Australia
4Department of General Surgery, Royal Prince Alfred Hospital, Camperdown, Australia
5Department of Surgery, The University of Sydney, Camperdown, Australia.
6IBM Research Europe, Dublin, Ireland

Advances in surgical near-infrared (NIR) cameras have allowed intraoperative use of indocyanine green fluorescence angiography (ICGFA) in breast cancer surgery for supplementary assessment of post-mastectomy skin flap perfusion, demonstrably reducing postoperative complication rates. Voluminous NIR recordings are ripe for computational interrogation, opening avenues to explore the role of artificial intelligence in developing surgical recommender systems. In this endeavour we aim to explore and parameterize a clinical case series of post-mastectomy NIR recordings into a dataset relevant for input into artificially intelligent algorithms.

Intraoperative post-mastectomy ICGFA videos from a retrospective case series collected at a single unit (2015 to 2022) within a clinical trial (Protocol X17-0359 HREC/17/RPAH/542) were obtained, organized, and visually assessed. Procedural data, patient demographics and postoperative outcomes were appended. Whole breast fluorescence time series data were extracted using a bespoke tracker/quantifier (IBM research) and arterial inflow milestones were extracted following annotation. Subsequently, machine learning (ML) algorithms were applied on an exploratory basis.

Recordings from patients (n=274 females) undergoing ICGFA, including primary and delayed (n=57) procedures were analysed. Videos with excessive movement, instrument intrusion, or missed inflow were discarded leaving 232 remaining (age range: 26-79 years, major complication rate: 10.3%). The digitized dataset was sufficiently robust to enable exploratory applications of ML algorithms yielding already promising results (sensitivity: 58.1%, specificity: 76.5%) for predicting postoperative complications.

Surgical video dataset curation is vital for developing artificially intelligent predictive models. This work achieved an acceptable standard for applying ML algorithms, encouraging further optimization and validation for intraoperative decision-support tools for cancer patients.

Prof Ronan A Cahill receives speaker fees from Stryker Corp, Olympus and Ethicon/J&J, research funding from Intuitive Corp and Medtronic and holds research funding from the Irish Government (DTIF) in collaboration with IBM Research in Ireland and from EU Horizon 2020 in collaboration with Palliare. Dr Jeffrey Dalli is employed as a researcher in the DTIF and is also a recipient of the TESS scholarship (Malta). Dr Jonathan P Epperlein is a full-time employee of IBM Research, a division of IBM, which provides technical products and services worldwide to government, healthcare, and life-sciences companies. Dr Chu Luan Nguyen and Sanjay Warrier report no disclosures.
Presenting Author:Abhinav Jindal
Supervisor:Prof Ronan A. Cahill
Co-Supervisor:Dr Jeffrey Dalli

p.bold: INVESTIGATING EPIGENETIC PROTEIN COMPLEXES IN HIGH-RISK CHILDHOOD BLOOD CANCERS

body.p: Arnott C1, Bhattacharyya T2, Bond J1, 2, 3
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Systems Biology Ireland, University College Dublin, Ireland
3Children’s Health Ireland at Crumlin, Dublin, Ireland

Polycomb repressive complex 2 (PRC2) is an epigenetic regulator of transcription. Genes encoding PRC2 factors are often mutated or deleted in childhood blood cancers and are linked to poor prognosis in paediatric acute myeloid leukaemia (AML).[1] We aimed to investigate these mutations’ impact on the complex.

We used a combination of computational tools that allow in silico prediction of PRC2 mutational effects on protein structure and function across AML and T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (T-ALL) cell lines. In-depth computational analysis of single nucleotide polymorphism in a PRC2 accessory component (AEBP2), seen in CCRF-CEM, a T-ALL cell line, and the resultant effect on its interaction with a PRC2 core protein (SUZ12) was investigated.[2] Molecular docking (HADDOCK webserver) was performed for both wild-type and mutant AEBP2 to SUZ12 wild-type, and Z-scores were calculated for the analysed clusters. Molecular dynamics simulations (GROMACS) were carried out for both complexes. To validate our in silico predictions, we performed immunoblotting of protein extracts from nine AML and four T-ALL cell lines, carrying differing PRC2 mutations (see table).

Our computational analysis indicates altered SUZ12-AEBP2 interactions in CCRF-CEM which carries mutant AEBP2, providing one example of affected complex structure (Z-scores = -1.6 (SUZ12-AEBP2 wild-type), -1.7 (SUZ12-AEBP2 mutant)). Overall, our analysis suggests that PRC2 mutations in patients can critically affect complex structure and function, impacting leukaemia biology.

Understanding PRC2 complex changes in childhood leukaemia reveals vulnerabilities, providing rational avenues for the development of new epigenetic-based cancer therapies which are increasingly being incorporated into clinical treatments.

list-item.p: Jones, L., McCarthy, P. and Bond, J. Epigenetics of paediatric acute myeloid leukaemia. Br J Haematol. 2020; 188: 63-76.
Chen S, Jiao L, Shubbar M, Yang X, Liu X. Unique Structural Platforms of Suz12 Dictate Distinct Classes of PRC2 for Chromatin Binding. 2018; 69(5): 840-52.

body.p: Chen S, Jiao L, Shubbar M, Yang X, Liu X. Unique Structural Platforms of Suz12 Dictate Distinct Classes of PRC2 for Chromatin Binding. 2018; 69(5): 840-52.
Presenting Author: Ceri Arnott
Supervisor: Prof Jonathan Bond

p.bold: CLINICAL AND GENOMIC CHARACTERISTICS OF YOUNG PATIENTS WITH MULTIPLE MYELOMA

body.p: Saldarriaga M1, Sowa A1,2, Jayabalan D3, Rosenbaum C1, Pearse R1, Monge J4, Niesvizky R1, Bustoros M1
1Division of Hematology & Medical Oncology, Weill Cornell Medicine, New York Presbyterian Hospital, New York, NY, USA
2UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
3Department of Pathology & Laboratory Medicine, Weill Cornell Medical College, New York, NY, USA
4Hematology & Medical Oncology, Weill Cornell Medical College/New York Presbyterian Hospital, New York, NY, USA

Multiple Myeloma (MM) is a plasma cell malignancy with a median age of 65-years at diagnosis. Within this study, we aim to understand the clinical characteristics and genomic drivers of young MM using the Weill Cornell/New York Presbyterian (WCM) and CoMMpass (MMRF) registries.
Using R Software (version 4.1), land-mark analysis was used to control time-dependent covariates within Kaplan Meier, Univariable, and Multivariable Cox proportional hazard models.

From the WCM registry, the <= 45-year group was enriched for individuals of African American descent (40% vs 10%, p = 0.04) and stage I ISS (51% vs 34%, p = 0.01). The median progression free survival (PFS) for each group was 7.92 years (95% CI 4.50 - NA) for the <= 45-year group and 3.25 years (95% CI 1.92 - 10.67) for the >45-year group. Within the MMRF study, the young group had similar trends as the WCM cohort plus a lower frequency of deletion 13q (35% vs 50%, p 0.03) and gain 1q (22% vs 42%, p < 0.01). DEA and GSEA showed a total of 1945 (3.5%) and 2164 (3.9%) over and under-expressed genes, with the young group being enriched for CD-1 transcriptional signatures (NES 1.86, adjusted p 0.028).

Young patients with MM have distinct clinical and demographic characteristics suggesting a potential role of genetic and environmental factors in early presentation. They also had a comparable to improved PFS than older patients potentially due to biologic drivers or factors related to patient age.
Presenting Author:Aubrie Sowa
Supervisor:Dr Mark Bustoros
Co-Supervisor:Dr Mateo Mejia Saldarriaga

p.bold: HAND HYGIENE COMPLIANCE, BARRIERS AND OPPORTUNITIES ACROSS HEALTH CARE FACILITIES IN ARUSHA, TANZANIA

body.p: Picard R1, Mwandri M2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Meru District Hospital, Arusha, Tanzania; Patandi Dispensary, Arusha, Tanzania

Infection transmission in healthcare facilities, known as Hospital Acquired Infections (HAI), play an enormous role in patient outcomes, hospital safety, and healthcare expenditures. Among the most cost-effective tools for controlling HAIs is hand hygiene [1]. Despite a high burden of HAIs in low- and middle-income countries, such as Tanzania, widespread use of hand hygiene practices has not been documented [2].

This study assessed hand hygiene compliance in community hospital and dispensary settings in Tanzania utilising quantitative and qualitative research methods. These included the WHO's Hand Hygiene Monitoring Tools as well as clinician focus group discussions. The study aimed to set a baseline on current hand hygiene standards in the facilities, with the goal of future improvement projects and subsequent assessment of the effectiveness of such interventions.

This research indicated that basic hand hygiene was not utilised in 91% of observed clinician-patient interactions. This is despite many of these clinicians displaying knowledge of hand hygiene, perceiving hand hygiene as high importance to patient outcomes, and having received formal hand hygiene training in the previous three years. During the observations with correct implementation, handwashing and hand rub were used at similar rates.

Although hand hygiene is a low cost and highly effective method to reduce HAIs, proper hand hygiene was chronically underutilised. The surveys, observations, and focus group indicated the greatest challenge was a severe lack of basic infrastructure. These findings are crucial to bring about quality improvement in the facilities’ hand hygiene, as well as for comparing healthcare facilities in Tanzania and beyond.

list-item.p: Habboush Y, Benham MD, Louie T, Noor A, Sprague RM. New York State Infection Control. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022. Available from: http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK565864/

Allegranzi B, Gayet-Ageron A, Damani N, Bengaly L, McLaws ML, Moro ML, et al. Global implementation of WHO’s multimodal strategy for improvement of hand hygiene: a quasi-experimental study. The Lancet Infectious Diseases. 2013 Oct;13(10):843–51.

body.p: Presenting Author: Rachel Picard
Supervisor: Dr Michael Mwandri

p.bold: REGULATION OF CXCL16 EXPRESSION IN DENDRITIC CELLS BY BACTERIA THAT COMMONLY COLONISE THE HUMAN RESPIRATORY TRACT

body.p: Alkanderi, AAHA1, Lalor, SJ1
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

Increasing evidence indicates that myelin-reactive T cells traffic through the airways in the pre-clinical stages of experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE), an animal model of Multiple Sclerosis. We have found that specific human respiratory tract bacteria including Moraxella catarrhalis and Klebsiella pneumoniae, but not Veillonella parvula, a bacterium commonly found in healthy human lungs, can promote pathogenicity in myelin-specific Th17 cells. This was associated with upregulated expression of the inflammatory chemoattractant molecule CXCL16 in the lungs of mice exposed to these bacteria as well as the recruitment of CXCR6-expressing Th17 cells to the lungs prior to their accumulation in the CNS. The cellular source of CXCL16 in response to bacterial stimulation is unknown.

We cultured murine bone marrow-derived dendritic cells (BMDC) for 10 days and confirmed their phenotype and maturation by flow cytometry, before exposing them to M.catarrhalis, K.pneumoniae and V.parvula. After 24 hours, we examined CXCL16 protein secretion in culture supernatants by ELISA and Cxcl16 gene expression by PCR.

We found that CXCL16 is constitutively produced by BMDC but were unable to detect any changes in chemokine concentration in cells following exposure to any bacterium. However, our data appeared to plateau, and future experiments will involve dilution of culture supernatants prior to ELISA. Nevertheless, we found that all three bacteria induced a dose-dependent increase in Cxcl16 gene expression, compared to untreated cells.
Our data indicates that bacteria colonising the human respiratory tract can promote CXCL16 expression in DC and may contribute to recruitment of pathogenic Th17 cells in inflammatory conditions.
Presenting Author: Amal Alkanderi
Supervisor: Dr Stephen Lalor

p.bold: THERAPEUTIC TOOLS FOR SPINAL CORD INJURY: INVESTIGATING THE POLARISATION OF BV2 MICROGLIA IN 3% GELMA HYDROGELS

body.p: Costello L1, Walsh C1, 2, Dooley D1, 2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Conway Institute of Biomolecular and Biomedical Research, University College Dublin, Ireland

Spinal Cord Injury (SCI) is very serious and debilitating condition that causes permanent motor and sensory dysfunction. There is currently no curative therapy available for SCI. Hydrogels are emerging as a promising drug delivery method as they can carry therapeutic factors and can be directly injected into the lesion site. By allowing for sustained therapeutic release, they can help modulate the neuroinflammatory response that occurs in the secondary injury phase of SCI. In this project, the focus was on determining whether GelMA hydrogels induce an inflammatory response in microglia, the resident immune cells of the central nervous system.

Sandwich ELISA and RT-qPCR were used to assess microglia polarisation (M1 and M2) markers. The hypothesis was that 3% GelMA hydrogels do not induce a significant inflammatory response in BV2 microglia over a 48 hour period. Statistical analysis included Shapiro-Wilk Normality Test, ordinary One-Way ANOVA, Dunnett’s Multiple Comparisons Test and Kruskal-Wallis test.

The results corroborated the original hypothesis; 3% GelMA hydrogels do not cause a significant change in the secretion of TNF-α & IL-10 from BV2 microglia at 24hr and 48hr. Furthermore, 3% GelMA does not cause a significant change in the expression of M1 markers (TNF-α and iNOS) nor M2 markers (Arg-1 and CD206) at 24hr and 48hr.

These findings suggest that GelMA hydrogels provide an effective platform for sustained drug delivery in a model of SCI. Future work aims to determine additional CNS cell responses to 3% GelMA hydrogels (e.g. neurons, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes) as well as its effectiveness in an in vivo rodent model of SCI.

list-item.p: Dooley D, Lemmens E, Vangansewinkel T, Le Blon D, Hoornaert C, Ponsaerts P, et al. Cell-Based Delivery of Interleukin-13 Directs Alternative Activation of Macrophages Resulting in Improved Functional Outcome after Spinal Cord Injury. Stem Cell Reports. 2016;7(6):1099-1115.

Walsh C, Wychowaniec J, Brougham D, Dooley D. Functional hydrogels as therapeutic tools for spinal cord injury: New perspectives on immunopharmacological interventions. Pharmacology & Therapeutics. 2022;234:108043.

body.p: Presenting Author: Louise Costello
Supervisor: Dr Dearbhaile Dooley

p.bold: FEARFULNESS RESPONSES OF THOROUGHBRED BROODMARES TO A NOVEL OBJECT

body.p: Rand A1, Moss A1, O’Reilly O1, Katz L1
1UCD School of Veterinary Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

The post-natal environment is dependent on maternal care style (MCS). MCS has been assessed in Arabians using fearfulness responses [1]. The aim was to test the hypothesis that fearfulness responses could be established in Thoroughbred (Tb) broodmares. The objective was to assess correlations between subjective behavioural scores and objective measurements of fearfulness.

Broodmares (n=25, 5-16 years old) were recruited from one farm: n=17 multiparous, n=8 ≤1 prior foal. Mares were instrumented with an ECG heart rate (HR) monitor and a 5-minute recording taken of the response to an umbrella opening. Two observers reviewed the videos, with measurements averaged and intra-assay coefficient of variations (CV) calculated (Table 1). Pearson’s correlation was used to assess associations between subjective and objective scores, age and parity (r≥0.4 correlation). Data is presented as mean ± standard deviation.

The mean startle response to the umbrella opening was 12.1±9.8 sec, with horses reaching a mean peak and average HR of 110.0±34.3 and 65.5±21.8 bpm, respectively. Mean separation and behavioural scores were 3.4±0.9 and 3.2±0.8, respectively. The startle response was moderately correlated to age (r=0.54), parity (r=0.65) and mean HR (r=0.42). Behavioural scores were moderately correlated to age (r=-0.53), parity (r=-0.55), startle response (r=-0.55), duration of touching object (r=0.61), peak HR (r=-0.58) and average HR (r=-0.60).

The results demonstrate correlations between objective and subjective scores of fearfulness to a novel object. Correlations between age, parity, the startle response, HR and behavioural scores may indicate increased experience with environmental stimuli. Associations of fearfulness with MCS need to be explored.
ItemDescriptionIntra-assay CV
Startle response (sec)Duration of the startle response to the umbrella. Begins when umbrella is deployed, ends when no signs of fearfulness for 5 sec.13.8%
Mare between object and foal (yes/no)Whether the mare places herself between umbrella and foal when it is opened.NA
Min distance to object (m)The closest the mare gets to the umbrella throughout the test.4.5%
Duration until touch object (sec)Duration of time until the mare touches the umbrella after the umbrella is deployed.5.6%
Frequency of touching objectNumber of times the mare touches the umbrella; a touch ends when the mare clearly stops touching the umbrella.0.81%
Duration touching object (sec)Duration for which the mare is touching the umbrella.6.0%
Exploring other stimuli (sec)Duration for which the mare explores other stimuli in testing area (foraging, nose below belly-line)17.9%

1 = high amounts of separation anxiety from herd
2 = moderate amounts of separation anxiety from herd
3 = mild amounts of separation anxiety from herd
4 = unbothered by being separated from herd8.8%

2 = timid, expresses anxiety about the object
3 = indifferent, does not react to the object
4 = calm, accepts the encountered situation
5 = friendly, playful, enjoying encountered situation13.8%

p.bold: EXAMINING THE PRESENCE OF OXIDATIVE STRESS IN INDIVIDUALS WITH POLYCYSTIC OVARIAN SYNDROME: A META-ANALYSIS

body.p: Terenzi DC1,2, Bakbak E2,3, Hess DA3,4,5 and Verma S2,3,6,7
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Division of Cardiovascular Surgery, St. Michael's Hospital, Toronto, ON, Canada
3Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada
4Molecular Medicine Research Laboratories, Krembil Centre for Stem Cells Biology, Robarts Research Institute, University of Western Ontario, London, ON, Canada
5Department of Physiology and Pharmacology, University of Western Ontario, London, ON, Canada
6Department of Surgery, University of Toronto, Stewart Building, Toronto, ON, Canada
7Institute of Medical Science, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada

Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome (PCOS) remains a challenging endocrine disorder, affecting up to 1 in 5 women of child-bearing age (1). Characterized by hyperandrogenism, oligomenorrhea and the presence of ovarian cysts, this syndrome is associated with multiple disease manifestations including infertility, type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease (CVD) (1).

The role of PCOS to the progression and manifestation of CVD remains unknown. Recently, the contribution of oxidative stress has gained attention as a major player in disease pathology, including CVD (2) due to its influence on DNA damage and cellular dysfunction (2). In this meta-analysis, the role of markers of oxidative stress known to contribute to the development of CVD are examined in individuals with PCOS compared to those without PCOS.

Following data accrual and extraction of 16 articles from PubMed and Google Scholar, the examination of markers such as glutathione and superoxide dismutase revealed that the antioxidant capacity of individuals with PCOS is significantly decreased compared to individuals without PCOS. Furthermore, pro-oxidant markers such as malondialdehyde were significantly elevated in individuals with PCOS compared to those without PCOS.

Therefore, current literature suggests a possible connection between the disproportional balance of oxidative stress in individuals with PCOS, consistent with observations in CVD. Consequently, the increased oxidative stress demonstrated in PCOS may play a role in the progression of CVD through atherosclerotic plaque development and dysfunctional cardiomyocyte contraction (2). Future studies should investigate the affected cellular microenvironment to better understand the role of oxidative stress and PCOS in the development of CVD.

list-item.p: Teede H, Deeks A, Moran LJ. Polycystic ovary syndrome: A complex condition with psychological, reproductive and metabolic manifestations that impacts on health across the lifespan. BMC Med [Internet]. 2010;8(41):1–10.

Fenkci V, Fenkci S, Yilmazer M, Serteser M. Decreased total antioxidant status and increased oxidative stress in women with polycystic ovary syndrome may contribute to the risk of cardiovascular disease. Fertil Steril. 2003;80(1):123–7.

body.p: Presenting Author: Danielle Terenzi
Supervisor: Prof Subodh Verma

p.bold: MOLECULAR DYNAMICS SIMULATIONS OF SARS-COV-19 OMICRON VARIANT PEPTIDES WITH MAJOR HISTOCOMPATIBILITY COMPLEXES

body.p: Vander Heyden K1, Riedel M2, Vasmatzis G3, Cornette J4, Udell J2, 3, Solanki A2, 3
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, MN 55455, USA
3Center for Individualized Medicine, Biomarker Discovery Group, Mayo Clinic, Rochester, MN, USA
4Iowa State University, Ames, IA, USA

Predictions of the binding strength of the immune system to viral peptides via major histocompatibility complexes (MHCs) is currently best done via neural networks1. This project sought to make these predictions from chemical interactions via molecular dynamics simulations.

The SWISS-MODEL software was utilised to obtain the Protein Data Bank (PDB) entry name for the desired MHC molecule. Alternatively, SWISS-MODEL found the most homologous crystallised MHC if the desired MHC’s structure was not crystallised. The CHARMM-GUI interface performed any required mutations to the entry’s amino acid sequence such that the file matched the target MHC sequence. Different peptide conformations were obtained by extracting bound peptides in PDB entries and mutating them to match the target viral peptide while retaining the original conformation. CHARMM software was later used to iteratively minimise the energy of the MHC-peptide complex to obtain a stable energy conformation2.

Once simulations were completed, the energy of how the peptide and MHC molecule interacted on a chemical level was recorded. The results of our simulations showed that molecular dynamics simulations can achieve both peptide conformations and interaction energy values representative of known structures. This suggests that viral peptide binding strength predictions relying on chemical interactions rather than neural networks are feasible.

This ongoing project will continue to develop and refine simulation techniques to ultimately predict the binding strength of novel virus peptides. Accurately making these predictions for a novel virus would enable the creation of a statistical model to predict a patient’s disease severity based on their MHC alleles.

list-item.p: Lundegaard C, Lamberth K, Harndahl M, Buus S, Lund O, Nielsen M. NetMHC-3.0: accurate web accessible predictions of human, mouse and monkey MHC class I affinities for peptides of length 8-11. Nucleic Acids Research. 2008; 36(2):W509-W512.
Brooks BR, Brooks III CL, Mackerel AD, Nilsson L, Petrella RJ, Roux B, et al. CHARMM: the biomolecular simulation program. J Comput Chem. 2009; 30:1545-1615.

body.p: Presenting Author: Kevin Vander Heyden
Supervisor: Prof Marc Riedel

p.bold: THE ASSOCIATION BETWEEN SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH AND VISION REHABILITATION OUTCOMES

body.p: Achanta P3, Rizzo JR1, Beheshti M2
1NYU Langone Health, New York, New York, USA
2Rusk Rehabilitation Center, New York, New York, USA
3UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

Social determinants of health (SDOH) are the non-medical factors, forces, and systems that shape conditions of daily life and influence health outcomes. SDOH have direct and indirect effects on lifestyle, health behaviors, and are linked to chronic diseases, such as diabetes and cardiovascular disease [1]. Research has classically focused on the association between SDOH and the acquisition of disease rather than the progression and outcomes of disease [2]. Furthermore, there is a large gap in scientific literature with respect to the relationship between SDOH and rehabilitation outcomes. In the present study, we consider SDOH and their associations with vision rehabilitation outcomes through literature review. Once granted access to data, our primary SDOH of consideration will include socioeconomic status (SES), ethnicity, access to health care, built environment including the quality of housing and vision-friendly environments, and access to transportation, all of which may modify rehabilitation outcomes for patients with visual impairments (VI). Vision rehabilitation outcomes of consideration include high level factors such as attendance, sustained adherence, and self-reported patient health status/ quality of life (QoL). In the future, we hope to extrapolate these findings to functional outcome measures of vision rehabilitation including improvements in oculomotor performance during eye hand coordination assessments. The present study is significant as having a better understanding of SDOH and their impacts on vision rehabilitation is crucial to informing rehabilitation services so they can best serve patients with VI.

list-item.p: Datto, A., n.d. Social determinants of health. [online] Who.int.

Bonnell, L., Crocker, A., Kemp, K., & Littenberg, B. (2021). The Relationship Between Social Determinants of Health and Functional Capacity in Adult Primary Care Patients With Multiple Chronic Conditions. The Journal Of The American Board Of Family Medicine, 34(4), 688-697

body.p: Presenting Author: Priya Achanta
Supervisor: Assoc Prof John-Ross Rizzo

p.bold: IN SITU HYBRIDIZATION FOR DETECTING BACTERIAL GENE EXPRESSION IN EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

body.p: Yacoob J1, Burke C2, Gallagher WM2, Rahman A1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland.
2Cancer Biology and Therapeutics (CBT) Laboratory, UCD Conway Institute of Biomedical and Biomolecular Research, University College Dublin, Ireland.

10-30% of those with early-stage, lymph node-negative breast cancer develop a recurrence following surgical resection [1] resulting in patients undergoing unnecessary treatment. Dysbiosis can be a contributing factor to the development of cancer. Pathogenic bacterial species, such as Fusobacterium nucleatum, have been proven to accelerate tumor growth and metastatic progression by promoting tumorigenesis. The aim of this study was to explore and validate the Hybridization Chain Reaction RNA-FISH (HCR RNA-FISH) assay [2] to investigate the expression of Escherichia coli (E. coli) in a quantitative and spatial context within the breast tumor microenvironment.

RNA-FISH detects the presence of specific transcripts by using nucleic acid probes linked to reporter molecules such as fluorophores. In this experiment, we attempted to validate the HCR RNA-FISH assay [2] using probes designed to target 16S E. coli rRNA. The results generated by the protocol were then compared to an already established protocol (RNAScope-Multiplex -Fluorescent-V2-assay) to assess its utility.

A high amount of background signal was associated with the protocol, in addition to unspecific staining in our negative mammalian cell line. Various experimental conditions were tested (incubation time, hybridization time etc.), however, the background signal did not improve. Our results indicated that this method requires fundamental improvement prior to widespread use in biomarker validation.

Therefore, it would be recommended to adopt the use of the RNAScopeV2 assay over the HCR RNA-FISH assay. The RNAScopeV2 assay has already been validated by the lab, and it holds great potential for future use in exploring prognostic biomarkers in breast cancer.

list-item.p: Lanigan F, Brien GL, Fan Y, Madden SF, Jerman E, Maratha A, et al. Delineating transcriptional networks of prognostic gene signatures refines treatment recommendations for lymph node-negative breast cancer patients. FEBS J. 2015 Sep;282(18):3455-73.

Schwarzkopf M., Liu M., Schulte S., Ives R., Husain N., Choi H., and Pierce N., 2021. Hybridization chain reaction enables a unified approach to multiplexed, quantitative, high-resolution immunohistochemistry and in situ hybridization. Development, 148(22).

body.p: Presenting Author: Javed Yacoob

p.bold: INVESTIGATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE IN A SEAL REHABILITATION FACILITY

body.p: Hung J1, Vale AP1
1UCD School of Veterinary Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

Gram negative bacteria found in the faecal microbiota of marine mammals are known to have both acquired and intrinsic resistance to many antimicrobials. Recently, acquired resistance to antimicrobials was identified in E. coli isolated from Irish grey seals (Halichoerus grypus) and harbour seals (Phoca vitulina) (Vale et al., 2021). With the rise of multidrug resistant Gram negative bacteria, treatment of infections is becoming more difficult jeopardizing animal and human health. This project aimed to characterize the antimicrobial resistance (AMR) profile of E. coli and other Gram negative bacteria isolated from seals’ faeces, water, fish soup and the environment at a seal rehabilitation facility.

Sampling was conducted on June 27, 2022 at Seal Rescue Ireland. Environmental samples were taken from 5 individual pens in use at the time, and 3 washed wet suits (surrogate mothers) using tissue swabs. Additionally, samples were taken from tap water, river water, 2 fish soups (26/06/22 and 27/06/22) and faeces from 2 seals. Water samples were processed through a membrane filtration unit with a 0.45µm pore size and membrane filters were either placed into Buffer Peptone Water (BPW) and incubated for 24 hours at 35ºC for total DNA extraction (Dallenne et al., 2010) or placed onto selective chromogenic media and incubated for 22 hours at 37ºC. Additionally, a 97 well Colilert system was used to detect and quantify both total coliforms and E. coli in water samples. Environmental swabs and fish soups were suspended in 90mL of BPW. Homogenized samples were then plated onto selective and chromogenic media: Tryptone Bile X-Glucoronice (TBX) (selective for E. coli), CHROMID® Colistin R Agar (selective for colistin resistant Enterobacteriaceae) and CHROMID® ESBL (selective for Extended-Spectrum Beta Lactamase-producing Enterobacterales) plates and incubated for 22 hours at 37ºC (bioMérieux Clinical Diagnostics, 2022).

Coliforms and E. coli were only detected in the river water sample with a most probably number (MPN) of 1986.3 for both. Twelve Gram negative isolates were recovered from CHROMID® ESBL plates and 15 Gram negative isolates including E. coli were isolated from CHROMID® Colistin R plates from environmental samples and seal faeces. Overall, 48 isolates were recovered.

Characterization of AMR in E. coli and other Gram negative bacteria within this population is important because seals are sentinel species that often reflect water quality/environmental contamination. The phenotype of isolates recovered from CHROMID® Colistin R and CHROMID® ESBL plates is of public health concern thus further investigation is ongoing.

list-item.p: bioMérieux Clinical Diagnostics. 2022. bioMérieux Clinical Diagnostics. [online] Available at: https://www.biomerieux-diagnostics.com[Accessed 5 August 2022].

Dallenne, C., Da Costa, A., Decré, D., Favier, C. and Arlet, G., 2010. Development of a set of multiplex PCR assays for the detection of genes encoding important β-lactamases in Enterobacteriaceae. Journal of Antimicrobial Chemotherapy, 65(3), pp.490-495.
Vale, A., Shubin, L., Cummins, J., Leonard, F. and Barry, G., 2021. Detection of blaOXA-1, blaTEM-1, and Virulence Factors in E. coli Isolated From Seals. Frontiers in Veterinary Science, 8.

body.p: Vale, A., Shubin, L., Cummins, J., Leonard, F. and Barry, G., 2021. Detection of blaOXA-1, blaTEM-1, and Virulence Factors in E. coli Isolated From Seals. Frontiers in Veterinary Science, 8.
Presenting Author: Jennifer Hung
Supervisor:Dr Ana Vale

p.bold: THE PREVALENCE AND SEVERITY OF BURNOUT AMONG MEDICAL STUDENTS AT UNIVERSITY COLLEGE DUBLIN

body.p: Ahmad M1, Donohue G1, O’Donnell B1
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

Academic burnout is an undesirable outcome that could arise from stressful university workload, which may cause emotional or social fatigue. 1 This study utilised the Maslach Burnout Inventory-Student Questionnaire (MBI-GS-S) to gauge the extent and the severity of overall burnout and on three subscales – emotional exhaustion (EE), cynicism (CY), and academic efficacy (AE) – among the preclinical medical students at University College Dublin (UCD).

This cross-sectional study utilised online and anonymous MBI-GS-S. It is a sixteen-item scale with three subscales marked on a seven-point Likert scale. A Higher score of AE indicates lower burnout, and higher scores of EE and CY indicate higher burnout. The questionnaire was conducted in a sample of 97 students (14.9% of UCD preclinical medical students) from different stages. Furthermore, a cluster analysis was conducted to categorise the students into appropriate burnout profiles.

There were 38 (39.6%) male and 58 (60.4%) female students. However, the mean scores of EE, CY, and AE were 4.28 ± 1.30, 2.83 ± 1.58, and 3.64 ± 1.11 consecutively; (range: 0-6). Overall moderate to high burnout was found in 45.36% of the students, and 29.90% being at high risk for burnout. It is important to note that the prevalence of burnout was 2-fold higher in international students compared to Irish students. However, gender was not significantly associated with any of the subscales of burnout.

This highly prevalent burnout level could possibly lead to a detrimental impact on the quality of life and the learning experience.2 Therefore, evaluating interventions to improve wellbeing is essential.

list-item.p: Galán F, Sanmartín A, Polo J, Giner L. Burnout risk in medical students in Spain using the Maslach Burnout Inventory-Student Survey. Int Arch Occup Environ Health [Internet]. 2011 Mar 04 [cited 2022 August 04];84(4): 453 – 459. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21373879/

O’Dea B, O’Connor P, Lydon S, Murphy A. Prevalence of burnout among Irish general practitioners: a cross-sectional study. Ir J Med Sci [Internet]. 2016 Jan [cited 2022 August 04];186(2): 447 – 453. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26803315/

p.bold: HANG UP YOUR CLEATS AND HOPE FOR THE BEST? A CROSS-SECTIONAL STUDY INVESTIGATING RETIRED ELITE FEMALE RUGBY PLAYERS’ HEALTH

body.p: Harrison C1, Thornton J2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Fowler Kennedy Sports Medicine Clinic, University of Western Ontario, London, Ontario, Canada

This study investigated the long-term health outcomes of elite, retired female rugby players. Minimal research has been conducted within the fields of sports medicine and acute/chronic injury prevention on this subject. Specifically, there is a disproportional lack of evidence on the lifelong physiological and psychological associations that contact-sport participation has on high-performance female athletes compared to their male counterparts [1].

Three consenting participants’ musculoskeletal, neurocognitive, reproductive/endocrine, mental and cardiovascular health were assessed to address this critical knowledge gap. A mixed-methods, cross-sectional design was implemented as participants completed an anonymized online self-reported questionnaire and a cognitive computer test. Various gold-standard academic survey questions were incorporated within the questionnaire, while the cognitive test assessed twelve essential neurocognitive domains.

Participants’ data provided a unique perspective on the long-term effects conferred by competitive sport. Their responses highlighted chronic musculoskeletal injuries, sports-related concussions (SRCs), anxious/depressive symptoms, and disordered eating habits. Unlike previous literature that has demonstrated the long-term neurocognitive ramifications of head collisions via contact-sport participation [2], no detrimental findings were indicated within each participant’s neurocognitive functioning. A major limitation was the study’s small sample size, not allowing for significant statistical analysis.

This preliminary study endeavoured to provide instrumental knowledge on the long-term impact that participation in international-calibre rugby has on retired female athletes. A larger-scaled longitudinal cohort study is essential to examine further the complex interrelations of contact-sport and the female athletic body. This will allow for the successful promotion and advancement of future safety guidelines/protocols, protecting current and future female athletes’ lifelong health.

list-item.p: Cowley ES, Olenick AA, McNulty KL, Ross EZ. “invisible sportswomen”: The sex data gap in sport and exercise science research. Women in Sport and Physical Activity Journal. 2021;29(2):146–51.

Cunningham J, Broglio SP, O'Grady M, Wilson F. History of sport-related concussion and long-term clinical cognitive health outcomes in retired athletes: A systematic review. Journal of Athletic Training. 2020;55(2):132–58.

body.p: Presenting Author: Chase David Harrison
Supervisor: Dr Jane Thornton

p.bold: DEVELOPMENT OF A BENCHTOP TEMPERATURE CONTROL SYSTEM FOR MICROGLIAL LIVE-CELL IMAGING

body.p: Alroqi M1, O’Carroll R1, Reynolds JP1 Dooley D1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Conway Institute of Biomolecular & Biomedical Research, UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury (SCI) is a significant medical problem worldwide, which diminishes quality of life and leads to severe functional impairments. SCI is characterised by the loss of motor, sensory and autonomic function below the lesion level that results from damage to and death of local neurons and glia. An initial primary injury phase exists, which is followed by a secondary, chronic neuroinflammatory phase, consisting of inflammatory cytokine release, axonal dieback and astroglial scar formation.1,2 Scratch wound assay (SWA) is a simple in vitro model of scar formation observed in SCI. However, current analysis methods are limited, with ‘before/after’ snapshots of cell density commonly used as a surrogate for migration.

Here we present a prototype live-cell optical imaging system, which captures cellular motion in real time over extended imaging trials. Briefly, BV2 microglia were maintained between 32-37 °C on the benchtop using a custom temperature control system and peristaltic pump to continuously circulate medium. pH, osmolality & flow rate were controlled for the duration of each experiment. Time lapse images were captured using a brightfield microscope.

In our optimized system, microglia did not display features of necrosis or apoptosis, i.e., no membrane blebbing, nuclear fragmentation or lysis, and remained viable and motile. Overall, this is a novel method for live-cell imaging on the lab bench, which allows longitudinal analysis at physiological conditions.

Further optimisation of this imaging system will be required, and additional data analysis techniques such as motion tracking and morphology analysis will be employed for a more comprehensive quantification of cell behaviour.

list-item.p: Rust, R. and Kaiser, J. (2017) Insights into the Dual Role of Inflammation after Spinal Cord Injury. The Journal of Neuroscience, 37(18), pp.4658-4660.

Dooley, D. et al. (2016) Cell-Based Delivery of Interleukin-13 Directs Alternative Activation of Macrophages Resulting in Improved Functional Outcome after Spinal Cord Injury. Stem Cell Reports, 7(6), pp.1099-1115.

body.p: Presenting Author:Mazen Alroqi
Supervisor:Dr Dearbhaile Dooley

p.bold: CHARACTERISATION OF EXOSOMES FROM PATIENTS WITH LYMPHANGIOLEIOMYOMATOSIS (LAM) AND TSC- /- MUTATED CELL LINES

body.p: Buckley G1, McCarthy C2, Jouida A2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Conway Institute of Biomedical and Biomolecular Research, University College Dublin, Ireland

Exosomes are extracellular vesicles between ~30 and 200nm in diameter1. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM) is a rare cystic lung disease characterised by abnormal growth of smooth muscle cells, lungs, lymphatic system and kidneys. Western blots using exosomes markers, CD63 and CD81, was done to confirm the presence of exosomes as well as to compare the expression of vimentin and E-cadherin in samples of A549 cells exposed to 50ug/ml of exosomes derived from either LAM or healthy samples. Nanoparticle analysis was carried out to determine the number of particulates per milliliter and zymography was preformed to measure the proteolytic activity of the matrix metalloproteinases, MMP-2, -9 and Pro-MMP-9.

The results found an increase in particulate number per milliliter in LAM samples (p=0.0273), a negative correlation between the particulates number per milliliter and forced expiratory volume 1% (p=0.0295, R=-0.4667, N=17), a positive correlation between particulates number per milliliter and VEGF level (pg/ml) (p=0.0496, R=0.4690, N=18), and an increase in particulates number per milliliter in patients without chylothorax (p=0.0185, N=3). There was an increase in proteolytic activity for Pro-MMP-9 in LAM samples (p=0.0278, N=18), and the A549 cells exposed to exosomes from LAM samples saw an increases in vimentin and E-cadherin expression, two markers of EMT2 (p=0.0108, P=0.0268). These results demonstrate the potential of exosomes as biomarker in LAM diagnosis. Further research could be done to determine the nature of EMT.

list-item.p: Z, N., A, M., A, E., Z, H. and R, S., 2022. Significance of E-cadherin and Vimentin as epithelial-mesenchymal transition markers in colorectal carcinoma prognosis. [online] PubMed.

Zhou, B., Xiang, J., Jin, M., Zheng, X., Li, G. and Yan, S., 2021. High vimentin expression with E-cadherin expression loss predicts a poor prognosis after resection of grade 1 and 2 pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. BMC Cancer, 21(1).

body.p: Presenting Author: Grace Buckley
Supervisor: Assoc Prof Cormac McCarthy
Kalusche M1, Doran P2, Holden S2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Clinical Research Centre, School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

Clinical trials are vital in delivering high-quality, safe, and effective healthcare. However, the healthcare sector is responsible for 4.6% of global greenhouse gas emissions (GHG) 1 and clinical research is responsible for a considerable carbon footprint, due to patient/staff travel, as well as resources for patient and onsite energy usage 2. There is a need to make clinical trials more sustainable. This study aims to quantify the carbon cost in clinical trials, to develop guidance for the design and running of clinical trials to minimise GHG.

This study is designed as a retrospective audit of trials conducted at the Clinical Research Centre (CRC). Data was collected from sources at the CRC, and included the trial protocol, trial master file and trial data. Carbon calculators were used to estimate the carbon associated with the trial elements; patient travel, trial team related travel, shipments of trial materials (including IMP), and energy usage onsite.

This study included five trials (only one was not randomised), with 111 participants and an average number of patient visits of 327. The trials resulted in an overall emission of 41.46 tonnes of CO2 (range=23.47, IQR=14.38). The largest contributor was patient travel, 41.43%, and the smallest contributor was the energy usage from onsite, 10.8%.

This study shows a change to the previous studies and demonstrates the impact of the pandemic on the design and conduct of clinical trials. Action can be taken to reduce the carbon footprint of clinical trials, such as moving to remote patient/monitoring visits.
The Clinical Research Centre would like to acknowledge the HRB-TMRN for their funding of this research.

list-item.p: WHO. Countries commit to develop climate-smart health care at COP26 UN climate conference nternet]. World Health Organisation; 2021 [cited 2022 Jul 26]. Available from: https://www.who.int/news/item/09-11-2021-countries-commit-to-develop-climate-smart-health-care-at-cop26-un-climate-conference

Lyle K, Dent L, Bailey S, et al. Carbon cost of pragmatic randomised controlled trials: retrospective analysis of sample of trials. BMJ [Internet]. 2009 Oct 10 [cited 2022 Jul 26] 339:b4187. Available from: https://doi-org.ucd.idm.oclc.org/10.1136/bmj.b4187

body.p: Presenting Author: Maeve Kalusche

p.bold: THE EFFECT OF STERILE INTRA-AMNIOTIC INFLAMMATION ON NEONATAL LIFE

body.p: Glazier C1, Farias-Jofre M2, Gomez-Lopez N2,3,4
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

2Perinatology Research Branch, Division of Obstetrics and Maternal-Fetal Medicine, Division of Intramural Research, Eunice Kennedy Shriver National Institute of Child Health and Human Development, National Institutes of Health, U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (NICHD/NIH/DHHS); Bethesda, Maryland and Detroit, Michigan, USA
3Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, Michigan, USA
4Department of Biochemistry, Microbiology and Immunology, Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, MI, USA

Preterm birth remains a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality worldwide. Preterm infants are at increased risk of both acute and long-term complications. The most well-established cause of spontaneous preterm birth is inflammation of the amniotic cavity, primarily sterile intra-amniotic inflammation (SIAI). The specific effects of SIAI on neonatal life have not previously been fully studied. Thus, our aim was to investigate the association between the perinatal outcomes of SIAI and the effects on neonatal life.

We used an animal model of SIAI previously established by our lab.1 Female C57BL/6 mice were mated with male C57BL/6 mice. Pregnant dams received ultrasound-guided intra-amniotic injection of IL-1α or phosphate-buffered saline as control. Rate of preterm birth, mortality at birth, neonatal survival and growth were recorded. Neurobehavioral testing was performed at 5 days of postnatal life and consisted of surface righting, cliff aversion, and negative geotaxis. Data analysis was performed using the Mann-Whitney U-test.

IL-1α injection increased the rate of premature birth (p=0.019) and mortality at birth (p=0.029). Exposure to intra-amniotic IL-1α was associated with reduced neonatal survival at 3 weeks of postnatal life (p<0.001) and reduced growth, with diminished weight (p=0.014) and biparietal diameter (p<0.001). Neurobehavioral testing did not reveal significant differences for either surface righting or cliff aversion, however a greater proportion of pups failed negative geotaxis in litters of dams injected with IL-1α (p=0.024).
Our results show that SIAI affects neonatal life. Future research will be focused on interventions to prevent adverse perinatal and neonatal outcomes associated with SIAI.

list-item.p: Motomura K, Romero R, Garcia-Flores V, Leng Y, Xu Y, Galaz J, Slutsky R, Levenson D, Gomez-Lopez N. The alarmin interleukin-1α causes preterm birth through the NLRP3 inflammasome. Mol Hum Reprod. 2020 Sep 1;26(9):712-726.

body.p: Presenting Author: Catherine Glazier
Supervisor: Assoc Prof Nardhy Gomez-Lopez

p.bold: ADENOSINE RECEPTOR SIGNALLING INCREASES MUCIN PRODUCTION IN THE INTESTINAL EPITHELIUM

body.p: Lee O1, Aljubury M1, 2, Stepanova M1,2, Aherne CM1, 2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Conway Institute of Biomedical and Biomolecular Research, University College Dublin, Ireland

The mucus gel layer (MGL) lining the intestinal epithelium separates epithelial cells from luminal contents. Early MGL disruption is observed in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) models and patients. Therefore, MGL dysfunction is thought to be important in IBD development. Strategies to improve MGL function are under investigation as novel IBD treatments. The mucin MUC2 is the major component of the MGL in the colon and ileum. The Aherne lab identified a novel protective role for the mucin MUC5AC in IBD. The lab also observed that the Adora2B adenosine receptor (A2BAR) protects the colon in murine IBD. Adenosine signaling has been demonstrated to increase mucin expression in lungs. Therefore, we hypothesised that adenosine signaling may increase mucin expression in the intestine.

T84 intestinal epithelial cells were treated with increasing concentrations of NECA, an A2 receptor agonist with varying specificity for A2B & A2A receptor (A2AAR). MUC2 and MUC5AC mRNA expression were measured using TaqMan RT-PCR. T84 cells were co-treated with NECA and A2AAR or A2BAR specific antagonist to determine the role of each receptor in mucin expression. Immunofluorescence with MUC5AC specific antibodies measured MUC5AC protein expression in A2BAR agonist-treated HT29-MTX-E12 cells.

NECA induces MUC2 and MUC5AC mRNA expression in a concentration dependent manner. NECA-induced MUC2 and MUC5AC mRNA expression was reduced to control levels with either the A2AAR or A2BAR antagonist suggesting NECA signals via both receptors to increase mucin expression. The A2BAR specific agonist induces MUC5AC protein expression.
Our findings suggest that A2AAR and A2BAR activation could promote MGL restitution.
Presenting Author: Lee Onn
Supervisor: Dr Carol Aherne

p.bold: POST MORTEM FINDINGS IN GREY SEALS (

body.p: Zhang H1, Saint-Marc A1, Imlau M2, Plamenova E2, Jahns H2
1UCD School of Veterinary Medicine, Pathobiology Section, University College Dublin Ireland
2Institute of Animal Health and Food Safety, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain

Establishing the cause of death of marine mammals such as stranded seals can provide valuable information on the wildlife population, changes of the coastal marine environment, and the presence of zoonoses including influenza and other infectious diseases of veterinary importance.

Post-mortem examinations were conducted on 53 grey seals and 13 harbour seals stranded between November 2019 and February 2022 along the Irish coast to determine the cause of death, monitor parasite burden and establish a comprehensive tissue bank for future research. Detailed history, signalment, clinical signs, and pathological changes found on necropsy were recorded. Parasites were identified morphologically under the microscope. A wide range of tissue samples was fixed in 10% formalin and frozen.

Stranded seals were mostly neonates/juveniles, with a majority of them found during their respective pupping season. The most common causes of death were septicaemia often secondary to conspecific trauma, followed by emaciation and parasitic bronchopneumonia. Less frequently identified causes included: other types of trauma, peritonitis, endoparasitism including heartworm, myositis, aspiration pneumonia, pyothorax, pneumothorax and haemoabdomen. Overall, the respiratory and alimentary tracts were the organ systems most affected. The main parasites found were nasal mites (Halarachne halichoeri) in 43.9% of seals, Anisakis nematodes in the stomach in 25.8% and lungworm (Otostrongylus circumlitus) in 22.7%.

The above findings are similar to those reported in seals in the North Sea and North Atlantic. The large numbers of conspecific trauma and marked parasite burden may suggest intra and inter species encounters generated/reinforced by population density and/or local geographic conditions.
The authors would like to thank Brian Cloak, Michael Dixon and Amanda Lawlor for their technical support. Thanks to the Irish Seal Sanctuary for providing the data and seals for this project.
Presenting Authors: Han Zhang
Supervisor: Dr Hanne Jahns

p.bold: EVALUATING THE EFFICACY OF THE COVID-19 BOOSTER VACCINE IN PRODUCING AN EFFECTIVE T-CELL RESPONSE

body.p: Barve A1, Cortázar J2, Mallon PWG2, Kenny G2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Centre for Experimental Pathogen Host Research (CEPHR), School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
T-cell immunity protects against COVID-19. However, the cellular response to the wild type (S) or Omicron spike (OS) peptides, induced by COVID-19 Booster vaccines, has not been fully elucidated.

Individuals recruited to the All-Ireland Infectious Diseases cohort study provided peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) before and 14 days after administration of a booster dose of BNT162b2. The PBMCs were stimulated with SARS-CoV-2 pooled peptides (S and OS), and the T-cell (CD4+ and CD8+) activation-induced markers were determined using flow cytometry. Pre- and post-booster responses were compared using a paired, two-tailed t-test. Data presented are mean (sd) unless otherwise specified.

3 individuals (median age 60) were analyzed. In terms of the CD4+ responses, there was no appreciable CD137+Ox40+ response to either peptides in the pre- or post-booster samples (SCD137+Ox40+:0±0.174vs0±0.125; OSCD137+Ox40+:0±0.129vs0±0.007). The HLA-DR+CD38+ post-booster response increased with S (0 to 0.005±0.003) but not with OS (0±0.011vs0±0.071). A PD1+ response to S was present pre- and post-booster (1.063±2.211vs0.533±2.967) but not to OS (0±0.978vs0±1.453). In the CD8+ subsets, the HLA-DR+CD38+ response to S decreased post-booster (0.048±0.198vs0.013±0.056) but not to OS pre- or post-booster (0±0.063vs0±0.019). The CD137+CD69+ response to S increased post-booster (0 to 0.072±0.117) but not to OS post-booster (0±0.411vs0±0.086). Lastly, the PD1+ response to S increased post-booster (0 to 0.049±0.288) but not to OS post-booster (0±0.648vs0±0.112). None of the increased responses reached statistical significance (all p>0.05).
No significant increase in CD4 or CD8 S or OS response was seen in these individuals post COVID-19 booster in this study. Larger cohorts will be required to confirm this finding.

list-item.p: McMahan K, Yu J, Mercado N, Loos C, Tostanoski L, Chandrashekar A et al. Correlates of protection against SARS-CoV-2 in rhesus macaques. Nature. 2020;590(7847):630-634.

body.p: Presenting Author: Arnav Barve
Supervisor:Dr Grace Kenny
Co-Supervisor: Prof Patrick Mallon

p.bold: IMMUNE RESPONSES OF HORSES TO CYATHOSTOMINS – SHEDDING LIGHT ON
INFLAMMATORY CONDITIONS OF THE GASTRO-INTESTINAL TRACT

body.p: Dunne S1, Mulcahy2, Walshe N1, Byrne O1, Nugent E1
1UCD School of Veterinary Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Conway Institute of Biomedical and Biomolecular Research, University College Dublin, Ireland
Cyathostomins are omnipresent among horses with over 40 species recognized as equine parasites.

Cyathostomin burden in horses exists predominantly as hypobiotic larvae within the mucosa of the large intestine. Sudden local and inflammatory responses, as well as gut microbiome dysbiosis known as acute larval cyathostominosis occur when hypobiotic larvae resume development and emerge into the gut lumen.

Cyathostomins pervasive effect on the immune system of horses remains unclear (1). The aim of this project is to shed insight on the immune response elicited by horses in response to these parasites and aid their management. Histopathological and immunohistochemically stained sections of intestine from horses with cyathostomins were examined to measure the contribution of different cell types and cytokines contributing to their response to infection. A multiplex cytokine ELISA assay measured secreted cytokines. Measurements obtained were correlated with data on burden levels and species of cyathostomin present in each horse.

Results demonstrated measurable levels of FGF-2, IP10 and TNF-a in the colonic and caecal mucosa of all horses. Consistently high levels of FGF-2 were present, not previously described. A tenfold increase of TNF-a in the clinical case was seen, with IL-1-b and GRO also measurable. A positive correlation between B-cells, T-cells, macrophages, and Fox P3 cells; IP-10 and FGF-2, and a putative association between fecal egg count (FEC) and Neutrophils, FEC and Fox-P3 was established. Concurrent molecular speciation of larvae illustrated only Cylicocyclus insigne present in the clinical case suggesting this species may elicit a more potent immune response, potentially TNF-a related and that re-emergence may be a species-specific event.

list-item.p: 5. Steuer A, Stewart J, Barker V, Adams A, Nielsen M. Cytokine and goblet cell gene expression in equine cyathostomin infection and larvicidal anthelmintic therapy. Parasite Immunology. 2020;42(6).

body.p: Presenting Author: Stacey Dunne
Supervisor: Prof Grace Mulcahy
Co- Supervisor: Dr Nicola Walshe

p.bold: MUSCLE ASYMMETRY AND LOW BACK PAIN

body.p: K. Clougher1, G. Cunniffe2, A. Pierce3, S. Morris1,2, 3
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2National Spine Injuries Unit, Mater Misericordiae University Hospital, Dublin, Ireland
3Beacon Centre for Orthopaedics, Dublin, Ireland

Low back pain (LBP) is a leading cause of productivity loss, with 223.5 million cases in 2019 worldwide1. Current theories highlight the cyclical nature of pain, inflammation, and muscle remodelling2. The aim of this project was to investigate recent literature on LBP, isokinetic strength testing, and muscle quality; and to develop protocols for isokinetic testing in LBP patients.

A review of recent literature was conducted, using the terms “back pain and muscle asymmetry”, “isokinetic testing hip”, “isokinetic trunk strength”, and “hip abductor strength, hip adductor strength”. Biodex hip testing protocols were assessed from the literature and the Biodex-4 user manual. Proof-of-concept testing was completed on project team member on the Biodex-4 at the Beacon Hospital Sports Lab.

Trends in the literature included: the cyclical nature of pain and muscle remodelling; interest in fatty infiltration distribution and extent; utilizing technology; and the potential of isokinetic testing to better understand injuries. Best practices for Biodex use should, in addition to manufacturer’s protocols, stabilize the free leg and provide patients a practice or learning session.

Views on LBP have evolved to include the role of pain and inflammation in perpetuating dysfunction and reducing muscle quality: poor muscle quality may be caused by pain, rather than vice versa. Fatty infiltration and isokinetic strength are promising targets for research, and have potential to improve both our understanding of LBP pathology and patient treatment plans. A promising avenue for future research is to establish a link between asymmetry in strength, quality, pain stage, and patient outcomes.

list-item.p: Wang L, Ye H, Li Z, Lu C, Ye J, Liao M, et al. Epidemiological trends of low back pain at the global, regional, and national levels. European spine journal. 2022;31(4):953-62.

Hodges PW, Danneels L. Changes in Structure and Function of the Back Muscles in Low Back Pain: Different Time Points, Observations, and Mechanisms. The journal of orthopaedic and sports physical therapy. 2019;49(6):464-76.

body.p: Presenting Author:Katherine Clougher
Supervisor:Prof Seamus Morris
Co-Supervisors:Dr Gráinne Cunniffe & Anthony Pierce

p.bold: INVESTIGATION OF FACTORS POTENTIALLY RELATED TO DEVELOPMENT OF MODERATE AND SEVERE DYSPHAGIA FOLLOWING ANTERIOR CERVICAL DISCECTOMY AND FUSION (ACDF) SPINE SURGERY

body.p: Curran J1,2, McDonnell J2, Cunniffe G2, Duggan S3, Gillivan-Murphy P3, Darwish S2, Butler J1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Belfield, Dublin 4
2National Spinal Injuries Unit, Department of Trauma & Orthopaedic Surgery, Mater Misericordiae University Hospital, Dublin, Ireland
3Speech & Language Therapy Department, Mater Misericordiae University Hospital, Dublin, Ireland

Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) spine surgery is a procedure used to treat cervical pathologies including traumatic, degenerative, and congenital disorders. Dysphagia, or difficulty swallowing, is a regular complication after ACDF [1]. The purpose of this research was to retrospectively investigate risk factors potentially related to moderate and severe dysphagia following ACDF surgery in the National Spinal Injuries Unit.

Patients who underwent ACDF surgery and were subsequently referred to Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) for evaluation due to dysphagia arising from their surgical operation were investigated in this study. A list of these patients, between 2015 and 2022, was gathered and used to build a study database. Patient demographics (example: age, gender), characteristics (example: comorbidities) and surgical notes (example: mean vertebral levels fused) were collated. Statistical methods were employed to identify if any risk or surgical factors played a contributory role for those experiencing dysphagia. Statistical analysis included independent t-test and Fisher’s exact test with p<0.05 noted as statistically significant.

Patients (n=53) were identified and classified as having either mild (n=7), moderate (n=31) or severe (n=15) dysphagia respectively. Post-operative tracheostomy was noted to be correlated with severe dysphagia incidence (p-value: 0.03), which agrees with existing literature [2].

Results also indicate a relationship between alcohol consumption and dysphagia, however, prospective studies with a larger sample size would be required to verify this association. Additional studies should also focus on recording full datasets to comprehensively test correlations between parameters. Severe dysphagia remains a rare but important complication to investigate and consider.

list-item.p: Liu, J., Hai, Y., Kang, N. Chen, X., Zhang, Y. Risk factors and preventative measures of early and persistent dysphagia after anterior cervical spine surgery: a systematic review. Eur Spine J. 2018 Oct 7; 27:1209–1218
Skoretz SA, Anger N, Wellman L, Takai O, Empey A. A Systematic Review of Tracheostomy Modifications and Swallowing in Adults. Dysphagia. 2020 May 6; 35:935-947

body.p: Presenting Author: Jack Curran
Supervisor: Dr Gráinne Cunniffe
Co-Supervisor: Prof Joseph Butler

p.bold: REDOX STRESS HANDLING IN NORMAL MELANOCYTES AND MELANOMA CELLS

body.p: Molloy G1, Casalou C2, Tobin D2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Charles Institute of Dermatology, University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland

The term “melanoma” describes a heterogeneous group of melanocyte-derived cancers. Although less common than keratinocyte-derived skin cancers, melanoma is more aggressive and treatment-resistant, and is thus a leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide. While the link between ultraviolet (UV) radiation and skin cancers is well established, oxidative stress is also thought to be a key driver of melanocyte transformation and melanoma progression. During melanin synthesis, a high number of reactive oxygen species (ROS) are generated, leaving melanocytes particularly vulnerable to this stressor. While brown/black eumelanin exhibits ROS-scavenging activity to counteract this redox imbalance, red/yellow pheomelanin lacks the same antioxidant capacity, leaving melanocytes in a highly pro-oxidative state [1].

The aim of this project was to compare the response to oxidative stressors – such as H2O2 and UV radiation – (a) in healthy melanocytes -vs- melanoma cells, and (b) among melanoma cell lines exhibiting differences in melanin content and subtype. High-mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) expression was used to measure oxidative stress. HMGB1 is a widely expressed protein which exerts different functions depending on its location within a cell. HMGB1 exists primarily inside the nucleus, where it acts as a DNA chaperone. Upon cell damage – e.g. oxidative stress – HMGB1 is released into the cytoplasm, then passively secreted into the circulation, where it acts as a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) [2].

The results of this project showed poorer redox stress handling in pheomelanin-rich melanoma cells, compared with eumelanin-rich melanoma cells. Furthermore, an increase in serum HMGB1 with disease progression in melanoma patients was observed.

The author would like to thank Prof. Desmond Tobin, Dr. Cristina Casalou, and Jay Mayatra at the Charles Institute of Dermatology for their guidance and contributions to this project. The author would also like to thank Dr. Shirley Potter and Dr. Stephanie Bollard at the Mater Misericordiae University Hospital for the contribution of patient samples.

list-item.p: Denat L, Kadekaro A, Marrot L, Leachman S, Abdel-Malek Z. Melanocytes as Instigators and Victims of Oxidative Stress. Journal of Investigative Dermatology. 2014;134(6):1512-1518.
Yu Y, Tang D, Kang R. Oxidative stress-mediated HMGB1 biology. Frontiers in Physiology. 2015;6.

body.p: Presenting Author:Grace Molloy
Supervisor:Dr Cristina Casalou
Co-Supervisor:Prof Desmond Tobin

p.bold: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF THE CLASSIFICATION OF GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT NEUROENDOCRINE NEOPLASMS IN STUDIES THAT UTILISE THE SURVEILLANCE, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND END RESULTS PROGRAM AND THE NATIONAL CANCER DATABASE

body.p: Hanson T1, Hua M2, Yaeger L2, Rodriguez JZ2, Hammill C2.
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Washington University School of Medicine, Washington University in St. Louis, Missouri, USA

Neuroendocrine neoplasms (NENs) are a rare, heterogeneous group of malignancies which affect neuroendocrine cells. Until recently, the system used to classify NENs varied across anatomical region and evolved constantly, causing confusion amongst researchers and clinicians. To resolve this, in 2018, the World Health Organisation developed a more consistent and uniform framework for classifying NENs. However, both the earlier and updated frameworks do not align well with how these tumours are categorised in national cancer registries such as the National Cancer Database (NCDB) and the Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results (SEER) Program. Based on this, it is possible that studies utilising these databases to research NENs have significant flaws in them.

This systematic review aims to determine whether the classification of neuroendocrine neoplasms of the gastrointestinal tract is applied correctly in scientific literature that utilise the NCDB and SEER.

A medical librarian performed a search of the Embase, Ovid, Scopus, CENTRAL, CDSR and Clinicaltrials.gov databases using the concepts of ‘neuroendocrine neoplasms’, ‘Surveillance Epidemiology End Results’, and ‘The National Cancer Database’. 1639 results were returned. 560 duplicates were removed. A researcher-performed screen of the remaining studies removed a further 819 studies and 42 duplicates.
Preliminary analysis found that in some studies, over 50% of NEN patients are included for analysis in the incorrect tumour designation.

Misuse of NEN nomenclature is significant as it has the potential to influence practitioners’ clinical decisions and affect patient outcomes. Further study is required to determine the extent of misclassification in the literature.
Presenting Author: Theodore Hanson
Supervisor: Dr Jacaranda Van Rheenen

p.bold: MISSED OPPORTUNITIES IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF TUBERCULOSIS MENINGITIS

body.p: Simmons N1,2, Olsen MA2, Reale J2, Bailey2, Mejia-Chew C2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Division of Infectious Diseases, Department of Medicine, Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis, Missouri, USA

Tuberculosis (TB) meningitis continuously has high morbidity and mortality. Early recognition and treatment is pivotal to improve prognosis. Declining TB incidence in the US has resulted in decreased clinical awareness, increasing potential risk of diagnostic delays. The objective was to determine the impact a missed opportunity (MO) for diagnosing TB meningitis has on 90-day mortality and cost of healthcare utilization. Secondarily, identify factors associated with a MO and predictors of 90-day mortality.

A Retrospective cohort of adult patients with central nervous system TB were identified in the Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project, State Inpatient and State Emergency Department Databases from eight states, using ICD-9 diagnosis code 013.X. MO was defined as signs, symptoms, and/or procedures suggestive of TB meningitis 6 months prior to the diagnosis. Univariate analysis of demographics, comorbidities, admission characteristics, and mortality compared between-group differences.

Of 893 patients with TB meningitis, 61.3% were male, 20.3% were white, 13.1% died within 90-days of diagnosis, and 44.0% had a MO. Comparing those with and without a MO, 90-day mortality (12.0% vs 14.0%; p=0.37) and mean cost of healthcare utilization ($83,416 vs $78,625; p=0.54) did not differ. 90-day mortality was higher with older mean age (60.1 vs 49.5; p=<.001), sepsis (41.9% vs 12.8%; p<0.001), and receiving mechanical ventilation (50.4% vs 12.4%; p<0.001) during the index admission.
Increased 90-day mortality was associated with older age, sepsis, and mechanical ventilation during the index admission for TB meningitis. Having a MO had no impact on mortality.
The authors would like to acknowledge the support of the University College Dublin Dean’s Scholarship.
Presenting Author: Niamh Simmons
Supervisor:Dr Carlos Mejia-Chew

p.bold: BARRIERS TO PELVIC EXAMS: WHAT STANDS IN THE WAY OF EMERGENCY MEDICINE PHYSICIANS?

body.p: Lennon Malbasha L1, Lostchuck E2,3, Scheuermeyer F2,3, Barbic D2,3
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
