2Department of Emergency Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, The University of British Columbia, Vancouver, BC, Canada
3Providence Health Care, Vancouver, British Colombia, Canada

People assigned female at birth (AFAB) presenting to the emergency department (ED) with possible gynaecological complaints such as pelvic pain and abnormal premenopausal bleeding should receive a pelvic exam as part of standard care.1 Although it is considered a critical exam, ED physicians do not consistently perform pelvic exams on patients presenting with relevant signs and symptoms.2 The present study includes a scoping literature review and the development of a survey aimed at understanding the barriers to conducting pelvic exams experienced by ED physicians.

The five-stage scoping review framework developed by Arksey, and O’Malley was followed2. An advanced literature search was carried out on EMBASE and PubMed using the key words ‘barriers to pelvic exam’ and ‘barriers to gynaecological exam’. Citation searching was also utilised.

Twelve studies were selected for review. The studies were broadly subdivided into barriers in general practice/primary care, the emergency department, and special populations. Barriers were separated into patient, physician and system barriers. Key barriers identified include physician gender, patient unwillingness, time pressure and equipment barriers. The results guided the development of a survey directed at emergency department physicians, with the aim of elucidating the barriers to conducting pelvic exams.

A pilot study for feasibility, survey clarity and readability, is currently in progress. The survey has been disseminated to Emergency Medicine physicians in British Columbia. Once survey responses have been returned, data analysis will begin. Phase two of the project will include a national study to explore larger trends around age, gender, and urban-rural trends.

list-item.p: Evans D, Goldstein S, Loewy A, Altman AD. No. 385-indications for pelvic examination. Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Canada. 2019 Aug 1;41(8):1221-34.

Mohart AE, Banet GA, Katz ED. Resident Sex and the Pelvic Exam: The Effect of Physician Sex on Selection and Management of Patients Who May Require a Pelvic Exam. Annals of Emergency Medicine. 2005 Sep 1;46(3):55-6.

body.p: Presenting Author: Louise Lennon Malbasha
Supervisor:Dr Emily Lostchuck

p.bold: RELIABILITY OF TWO EQUINE BODY CONDITION SCORING SYSTEMS

body.p: Fox E1, Walshe N2, Duggan V2, Golding E2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD School of Veterinary Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

Obesity in horses is increasingly common and causes health problems like equine metabolic syndrome, musculoskeletal problems, and laminitis, which can be fatal. Body condition scoring is used to monitor a horse's weight quickly and simply and can be done by owners and vets. It provides insight into the horse’s wellbeing and its risk of developing weight related conditions

During this study 5 experts in equine health were asked to score 17 horses. They used 2 different systems, the Henneke and the Carroll & Huntington, each of which were repeated for a total of 4 scores for each horse by each assessor. This allowed an evaluation of repeatability (the same assessor assigns the same score to the same horse in the same condition more than once) and reproducibility (2 or more assessors assign the same score to the same horse in the same condition).

Results showed moderate to substantial intra-assessor agreement (Henneke: κ = .59 - .71, P < .001; Carroll & Huntingdon: κ = .64 - .66, P < .001) while inter-assessor agreement is good (Henneke: ICC = .78, P < .001; Carroll & Huntington: ICC = .76, P < .001) for both systems. This indicates that there is relatively good repeatability and reproducibility in equine body condition scoring, despite the subjective nature of the systems.
The results of this study will indicate the validity of the use of equine body condition scoring. This may affect the use of body condition scoring in clinical decisions and weight loss programmes.
Presenting Author: Ellen Fox
Supervisor: Ms Emma Golding

p.bold: HOSPITAL ACQUIRED PNEUMONIA: 1 YEAR FOLLOW AUDIT TO ASSESS IMPACT OF HOSPITAL ACQUIRED PNEUMONIA PREVENTION CARE BUNDLE

body.p: Chee E1, Bright C1, Leahy D2, Linnane S1, 2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Beacon Hospital, Respiratory Department, Dublin, Ireland

Hospital Acquired Pneumonia (HAP) is a nosocomial infection occurring in hospital patients 72 hours post admission. HAP has not been a focus for research due to its difficulties in prevention and diagnosis. HAP occurs frequently and often results in prolonged hospital stay, increased mortality, patient morbidity and higher healthcare cost.

The hospital database was searched for descriptions consistent with diagnosis of HAP. 1460 patients were identified and further analysed for clinical criteria consistent with HAP. 70 patients fit the criteria, 7 of which had COVID-19 during their stay which are excluded in this study. Variables examined were consistent with the baseline audit including white cell count, C-reactive protein (CRP) levels.

Risk factors for HAP are consistent with the baseline audit including low haemoglobin levels on admission (38 patients), presence of active cancer (20 patients), presence of cardiac disease (32 patients) as well as adverse outcome including high CRP levels and white cell count.

A HAP bundle was introduced following the baseline audit, as a result, HAP incidence rate has reduced (from 70 to 63), decreased severity of HAP can be seen from the declining average highest CRP levels (from 190.64 to 185.25). Number of ICU patients who were later diagnosed with HAP has trebled (from 8 to 21) however outcome has considerably improved with shorter length of stay, lower average highest CRP levels and ICU patient death.
Presenting Author: Ethan Chee
Supervisor: Dr Seamus Linnane
Co-Supervisor: Ms Donna Leahy

p.bold: OBSERVATIONS OF A LONG COVID CLINIC: EFFECTS ON LUNG FUNCTION

body.p: Chang G1, Kalyansundaram K1, Dickson J1, Linnane S1
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

The Long COVID condition affects an estimated one in 10 people infected with SARS-Cov-2 (COVID-19).1 Despite a significant proportion of the population suffering from this condition, there is a very limited understanding of the pathophysiological effects of this condition towards lung function. This study aims to identify trends of Long COVID that may be helpful for future clinical reference and possible research.

This observational study looks at patients from the Beacon Hospital Long COVID outpatient clinic. Along with physiological tests, including pulmonary function tests (PFTs), questionnaires were sent out to each patient that required patients to detail their current medical status through SF36, HADS and Chandler fatigue scores. The patient’s data was then tabulated and analysed for correlation between symptoms and physical performance scores. Further analysis involved grouping patients based on age and gender was done to assess potential trends within each group.
SexNumberDLCO/VA%MEPS%MIPS%AnxietyDepressionMMRC ≥2
Male5599.8679.8483.247.677.6721 (38%)
Female1078884.3275.239.268.0860 (56%)
Analysis of the data showed trends towards anxiety and depression in this group (overall >55% scored mild – severe anxiety /depression). Shortness of breath (MMRC ≥2) was reported in 50% of patients.
Correlation analysis did not show any link between results of questionnaires and physical assessment across genders or age groups presenting.

There was a high variability of symptom presentation of each individual patient presenting to clinic and we were unable to identify any pattern between symptom presentation and physical deficits in this Long COVID patient cohort. Further research looking at symptoms and physical exam considering different phenotypes of presentation may be more effective in identifying underlying pathologies in Long COVID patients.

list-item.p: World Health Organisation. ‘In the wake of the pandemic: Preparing for Long COVID’ Policy Brief, No.39. Copenhagen, Denmark: WHO Regional Office for Europe, 2021.

body.p: Presenting Author: Geoffrey Chang
Supervisor: Dr Seamus Linnane

p.bold: CHARACTERISATION OF DECEASED PATIENTS WHOSE BLOOD SAMPLES WERE SENT TO THE MATER SUDDEN ADULT DEATH SYNDROME (SADS) BIOBANK

body.p: Brennan S1, McGorrian C2, Galvin J2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Family Heart Screening Clinic, The Mater Misericordiae University Hospital, Dublin, Ireland

SADS refers to sudden death in the setting of a completely normal autopsy [1]. This research analysed autopsy reports of deceased individuals whose samples were sent to the Mater SADS Biobank, determining how many of these samples (a) are appropriate for molecular autopsy, (b) require screening of respective family members and (c) may be discarded.

Referring pathologists were asked to forward individual autopsy reports. These reports were systematically recorded following a defined data set focusing on details of lifestyle factors, pathology, cardiac anatomy, histology, toxicology and extracardiac causes of death.

Agreement between two reviewers regarding a cardiologist adjudicated (a) cause of death and (b) need for gene panel analysis was investigated. Regarding (a) cause of death, there was substantial interobserver agreement: Cohen’s Kappa = 0.73 (77.5% agreement, p<0.001). Both reviewers agreed that SADS, followed by coronary artery disease, followed by sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP) were the most common causes of death. In total 14 SADS cases were identified: 10 by reviewer 1 (25%) and 13 by reviewer 2 (32.5%). Regarding (b) need for gene panel analysis, there was near perfect agreement: Cohen’s Kappa = 0.85 (92.5% agreement, p<0.001). Reviewer 1 identified 24 cases for gene panel analysis (60%), with reviewer 2 agreeing with 21 of these 24 cases (52.5%).

Post-mortem review of SADS biobank samples is essential to determine whether (a) family screening and (b) gene panel analysis is required. Of n=40, 60% of cases were deemed suitable by at least 1 reviewer for gene panel analysis.

list-item.p: Basso C, Aguilera B, Banner J, Cohle S, d’Amati G, de Gouveia RH, di Gioia C, Fabre A, Gallagher PJ, Leone O, Lucena J. Guidelines for autopsy investigation of sudden cardiac death: 2017 update from the Association for European Cardiovascular Pathology. Virchows Archiv. 2017, 471(6):691-705.

body.p: Presenting Author: Sophie Brennan
Supervisor: Prof Joseph Galvin

p.bold: DETERMINING THE PREVALENCE AND NATURE OF CRITICAL INCIDENTS IN CHILD AND ADOLESCENCE MENTAL SERVICES: A SCOPING REVIEW

body.p: Dharmaraj K1, Hayden J2, Zahari Ashraf A1, McNicholas F1, Gavin B1
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2School of Pharmacy and Biomolecular Sciences, Royal College of Surgens Ireland, Dublin, Ireland

The reporting of critical occurrences is a vital part of clinical management and is crucial for patient safety, safety of staff and healthcare quality assurance. Incident reporting includes the detection, reporting and analysis of events with the potential for harm. The aim of this scoping review was to identify and describe in existing literature the nature and prevalence of critical incidents within child and adolescent mental health services.

The scoping review followed Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) methodology. Three electronic databases were searched and reported its findings according to PRISMA guidelines. The search strategy included all youth under 18. The context included any child mental health setting and services provided. The concept focused on critical incident reporting, any adverse event or near misses. For quality assurance a second reviewer screened 50% of the total 3494 studies. Followed by a full text review of 52 studies retrieved, with 6 studies charted.

Findings in one study suggested, the most common acts of critical incidents were seen to be staff assault, physical aggression, verbal assault, and threats.1 Additionally, 2/6 of the selected studies reported a vast majority of critical incidents involved the same cohort of youth. For instance, (n =164/167; (98%)) had been involved in 70 or fewer incidents, (n=3/167 (2%)) were involved in more than 100 each.2

Current findings indicate critical event reporting has promising benefits, such as assisting staff in analyzing current management practice. Further research is needed to develop policies and supports in primary and secondary child psychiatric healthcare systems.

list-item.p: O'Keeffe N, Gavin B, McNicholas F. Survey on critical incident reporting in a child and adolescent mental health service. Int J Risk Saf Med. 2013;25(1):33-8.
Boxer P. Aggression in very high-risk youth: examining developmental risk in an inpatient psychiatric population. Am J Orthopsychiatry. 2007;77(4):636-46.

body.p: Boxer P. Aggression in very high-risk youth: examining developmental risk in an inpatient psychiatric population. Am J Orthopsychiatry. 2007;77(4):636-46.
Presenting Author:Krithika Dharmaraj
Supervisor: Assoc Prof Blánaid Gavin
Co Supervisor: Prof Fiona McNicholas

p.bold: SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF REFERRALS TO CHILD AND ADOLESCENT MENTAL HEALTH SERVICE (CAMHS) DURING COVID-19

body.p: Yap S1, Lynch F2, McNicholas F1, 2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Saint John of God Community Mental Health Services, Lucena Clinic, Dublin, Ireland

There has been an increase in mental health presentations of youth to child and adolescent mental health services (CAMHS) following the Covid-19 pandemic. The purpose of this research is to examine pattern of referrals pre Covid-19 (2019) and post Covid-19 (Mar 2020- Jun 2022) and examine whether initial changes in 2020 have persisted. Following Ethics approval, all referrals to one geographically defined CAMHS catchment area were identified between March 2019 to June 2022. Clinical data was extracted from the electronic health records and anonymised for data entry and analysis

This study showed that in all accepted referrals, the mean age of the cohort over the years was 13 where females constituted majority of the referrals in post-Covid-19. There were significant increases in thoughts of self-harm (TSH)/suicidal ideation (SI), deliberate self-harm, Anxiety Disorders and Eating Disorders. The number of TSH/SI increased with each consecutive year after Covid-19. Referrals with concerns of possible mood disorder maintained pre-Covid base line levels while ADHD/ASD and behavioural disorder have fluctuated. Majority of accepted referrals throughout the years presented with internalising disorders with an average of 68% while externalising disorders presented with an average of 21%.

Number of referrals and accepted referrals/month had increased during the post Covid-19 period vs pre Covid-19. Given the sustained increase in referrals to CAMHS it is likely that patient waiting lists will begin to rise with resultant negative impact on youth mental wellbeing, in addition CAMHS’s staffs will also be more susceptible to burnouts due to increased work load.
Presenting Author:Sean Joash Yap Jun-U
Supervisor:Prof Fiona McNicholas
Co-Supervisor:Dr Fionnuala Lynch

p.bold: EMERGENCY MENTAL HEALTH PRESENTATIONS TO CHILDREN’S HEALTH IRELAND (CHI) AT CRUMLIN DURING COVID-19

body.p: Sun B1, Moore K2, McNicholas F1, 2, 3
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Department of Psychiatry, Children’s Health Ireland at Crumlin, Dublin, Ireland
3Lucena Clinic Rathgar, St John of God Hospitaller Services, Dublin, Ireland

Following COVID-19 and associated restrictions, there has been an increase in psychological distress among youth in Ireland, with an increase of help-seeking to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) [1]. Hospital psychiatric admissions across five pediatric hospitals showed a 28.3% increase above prior-year levels in 2020 as initial lockdown restrictions were eased [2].

This study extends initial findings by examining the current emergency psychiatric presentations in CHI Crumlin Hospital in 2022 as COVID-19 becomes endemic and compare to the same time frame in 2021.

This is a retrospective cohort study covering two six-month periods from 1st January 2021 to 30th June 2021, and 1st January 2022 to 30th June 2022. Following ethical approval, data extraction from charts of patients with emergency psychiatric presentations were conducted. Data were compared using Welch’s t-test and chi-square tests.

A 30.2% decrease in number of acute psychiatric presentations in 2022 (120) were observed compared to 2021 (172). Presentations outside of normal working hours significantly decreased (60.5% vs 25.8%, p<0.001) as did onward discharge to CAMHS (87.8% vs 80.8%, p<0.001). With a non-significant increase of ADHD (20.3% vs 28.3%, p=0.114), a significant increase of youth on psychotropic medications (28.5% vs 33.3%, p=0.001) were observed.

The majority of clinical presentations were similar across timeframe with approximately 55% of suicidal ideation, 63% of deliberate self-harm, and 60% of depression or anxiety. Ongoing auditing of emergency psychiatric presentations is needed to examine whether the reduction observed in 2022 will continue.

The author would like to acknowledge the opportunity, time and guidance given from Prof. Fiona McNicholas, Dr. Kirean Moore, Dr. Kristen Maunder, Ms. Elisha Minihan and all members in CHI Crumlin psychiatry department.

list-item.p: McNicholas F, Kelleher I, Hedderman E, Lynch F, Healy E, Thornton T et al. Referral patterns for specialist child and adolescent mental health services in the Republic of Ireland during the COVID-19 pandemic compared with 2019 and 2018. BJPsych Open. 2021;7(3).

McDonnell T, Conlon C, McNicholas F, Barrett E, Barrett M, Cummins F et al. Paediatric hospital admissions for psychiatric and psychosocial reasons during the first year of the COVID-19 pandemic. International Review of Psychiatry. 2022;34(2):128-139.

body.p: Presenting Author:Bohan Sun
Supervisor:Dr Kirean Moore
Co-Supervisor:Prof Fiona McNicholas

p.bold: IMPACT OF SUMOYLATION INHIBITOR (TAK-981) TREATMENT ON SARS-CoV-2 NUCLEOPROTEIN MULTIMERIZATION

body.p: Al Ghammari M1 McLoughlin LE2, Gautier V2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Centre for Experimental Pathogen Host Research (CEPHR), School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

The COVID-19 pandemic has led to numerous ongoing consequences. However, the treatment options against SARS-CoV2 are still limited. Thereof, it is necessary to identify novel targets for drug development at the host-virus interface. Nucleocapsid protein (NP) is one of the structural proteins of the virus. NP multimerises and binds the viral RNA to form large ribonucleoprotein complexes during virion formation and packaging. A previous study in the lab has shown that NP undergoes SUMOylation, a post-translational modification in which SUMO (small ubiquitin-like modifier) protein is attached to lysine residues[1]. SUMOylation is fundamental in regulating protein-protein interactions, protein activity, stability, and translocation[2]. This study aimed to optimise formaldehyde fixation conditions to observe large NP multimers in the crosslinked samples, and to examine whether SUMOylation is involved in the multimerization of NP.

24 hours post-lipofectamine transfection with Flag-NP and HIS-SUMO-2, HEK293T cells were incubated with TAK-981 (50µM) for 5 hours. Next, they were fixed with 4% formaldehyde for 5 minutes and lysed with 4x Laemmli sample buffer.

Compared to control samples, western blot analysis of the treated conditions showed a 162% increase in the intensity of single NP bands concomitant with a 58% reduction in the intensity of large NP multimer bands (>300kDa). Reverse crosslinking confirmed equal amounts of NP in treated and control conditions.

The results show interference of NP multimerization in TAK981-treated cells, which implies that SUMOylation is likely to be involved in NP multimerization. This may set the basis for future drug development and biomolecular research related to SARS-CoV-2.

list-item.p: McLoughlin LE. The SARS-CoV-2 Nucleocapsid Protein: A Target for SUMOylation [Research Masters]. University College Dublin; 2021.
Geiss-Friedlander R, Melchior F. Concepts in sumoylation: a decade on. Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology. 2007; 8(12):947-956.

body.p: Presenting Author: Mohamed Al Ghammari
Supervisor: Assoc Prof Virginie Gautier
Co-Supervisor: Leah Ennis McLoughlin

p.bold: DEVELOPING AN INFORMATION RESOURCE FOR PATIENTS UNDERGOING BARIATRIC SURGERY EXPLAINING THE IMPORTANCE OF ACTIVE MANAGEMENT OF IRON STATUS AFTER SURGERY

body.p: Narang R1, Gletsu-Miller N2, Docherty N1
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Indiana University School of Public Health, Bloomington, IN, USA

Bariatric surgery is a highly effective and safe treatment option for obesity. However, anatomical rearrangements to the stomach and small intestine can impair assimilation of dietary micronutrients. Hence, bariatric surgery confers a risk for the development or progression of a range of nutritional deficiencies [1]. Iron deficiency (ID) is common in bariatric surgery patients and frequently worsens following surgery in the absence of effective high-dose supplementation strategies [1]. Iron supplementation can also be poorly tolerated, and alongside an underappreciation of the consequences of ID, results in more than half of patients becoming iron deficient within 3 years of surgery [2]. The aim of this study was to develop an information resource for patients undergoing bariatric surgery and enhance medical literacy on post-operative management of bariatric surgery with respect to ID.

Here, an infographic will be created to aid the understanding and compliance of bariatric patients to iron supplementation. Following quantitative and qualitative feedback gathered from a focus group, the infographic will be finalized and distributed to multiple social media platforms of obesity related societies, bariatric surgery services and medical information hubs.

The focus group will consist of a brief presentation with knowledge-based questions before and after to compare the improvement of understanding of participants. Qualitative feedback gathered from participants will further improve the final infographic.

As a result, an appealing infographic catering to the general population will serve to improve medical literacy on post-bariatric surgery complications. Furthermore, prevention and treatment modalities for post-operative ID will be provided to potential bariatric surgery candidates.

list-item.p: Smith C, Herkes S, Behrns K, Fairbanks V, Kelly K, Sarr M. Gastric Acid Secretion and Vitamin B12 Absorption After Vertical Rouxen-Y Gastric Bypass for Morbid Obesity. Annals of Surgery. 1993;218(1):91-96
Vargas-Ruiz A, Hernández-Rivera G, Herrera M. Prevalence of Iron, Folate, and Vitamin B12 Deficiency Anemia After Laparoscopic Roux-en-Y Gastric Bypass. Obesity Surgery. 2008;18(3):288-293

body.p: Presenting Author: Rakesh Narang
Supervisor: Assoc Prof Neil Docherty

p.bold: TRANSITION TO CLINICAL PRACTICE: EXPERIENCES OF INTERNATIONAL STUDENTS

body.p: Lee KE1, Higgins M2, Farran M2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Perinatal Research Centre, University College Dublin, National Maternity Hospital, Dublin, Ireland

Transitions can be defined as periods of change. Transitions in medical education are potentially complex, emotionally and socially dynamic. Three different types of transitions in medical education which are traditionally described: transition from preclinical to clinical training, from student to specialty training and from trainee to trained doctor. The aim of this study was to explore the transition of medical students from preclinical to clinical training, specifically from international students’ perspective.

The most appropriate research method was a scoping review, which was conducted following the Arksey & O’Malley and PRISMA guidelines. The most appropriate aim for this scoping review was “to examine the extent, range and nature of research activity”. The five steps of scoping review were completed including identifying the research question, identifying the relevant studies, study selection, charting the data, and lastly, collating, summarising and reporting the results.

Following a systematic search of PubMed, Embase and Scopus from 2010 to 2022, twelve full test papers met the criteria for review. Four general themes about the transition from preclinical to clinical training were identified: workload/time, patient contact, team relationship and gap in knowledge and skills. No research study compared the experiences or perceptions of international students compared to native medical students.

Future studies may explore possible differences between international and native medical students. We hypothesised that international students may have additional challenges in the transition but could not find any evidence of this being explored in the published medical education literature.
(246 words of 250 permitted)
Presenting Author: Kai En Lee
Supervisor: Dr Mary Higgins
Co-Supervisor: Dr Maria Farran

p.bold: TRANSITION OF MEDICAL STUDENTS FROM PRE-CLINICAL TO CLINICAL EDUCATION: A SCOPING REVIEW FOCUSSING ON GENDER DIFFERENCES

body.p: Soobratty HF1, Farren M1,2, Higgins MF1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Perinatal Research Centre, University College Dublin, National Maternity Hospital, Dublin, Ireland

Medical students often describe the transition from the pre-clinical to clinical years as being challenging due to the numerous hurdles which need to be overcome in order to adapt to the new environment. The objective of this study is to identify any possible gender differences in this transition experience.

This scoping review follows the six-staged Arksey and O’Malley framework and aims to “examine the extent, range and nature of research activity” on the topic.1 The PRISMA-ScR guidelines were followed to ensure completeness. The databases PubMed, Scopus and Embase were searched for the date filter: January 2010 to May 2022. A decision was made to restrict the search results to six journals with high impact factor, as was performed by Teunissen and Westerman in their earlier review.2

The database search yielded 179 results. After reviewing titles, abstracts and full-texts, 11 articles were found relevant to this transition, of which two articles discussed gender differences. Studies demonstrated higher levels of self-reported anxiety, stress and depression from female students as opposed to their male counterparts. In comparison, males exhibited stronger feelings of excitement and confidence regarding the transition. A decrease in level of empathy in patient care was also observed among female students during the transition while the level for males remained steady.

The findings demonstrate that there exist gender differences, particularly with respect to the psychological impact of the transition. The results are however limited. Hence, the use of more structured psychological scales and diverse gender inclusion are potential future research avenues.

list-item.p: Arksey H, O'Malley L. Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework. International journal of social research methodology. 2005;8:19-32
Teunissen PW, Westerman M. Opportunity or threat: the ambiguity of the consequences of transitions in medical education. Medical education. 2011;45:51-59

body.p: Presenting Author: Hayfa Fatima Soobratty
Supervisor: Dr Mary Higgins
Co-Supervisor: Dr Maria Farren

p.bold: TRANSITIONS IN MEDICAL EDUCATION: A SCOPING REVIEW FOCUSING ON THE TRANSFORMATION FROM DOCTOR IN TRAINING TO SPECIALIST

body.p: Ahmed M1, Farren M1,2, Higgins MF1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Perinatal Research Centre, University College Dublin, National Maternity Hospital, Dublin, Ireland

A sparsely documented phenomenon, the transition to consultant is an immensely important transformation in the career of any medic and one that carries arduous burdens. The objective of this study was to highlight the biggest barriers and impediments to achieving consultancy status in medicine.

Conducted in the style of a scoping review, this study followed Arksey H, O'Malley’s framework, producing “a synthesis of existing knowledge in the aim to map rapidly key concepts underpinning a research area”1. Embase, Pubmed and Scopus catalogues were searched from 2010 till 2022 with a restriction to six publications highlighted by Teunissen and Westerman2. The search process was also guided by the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and MetaAnalyses (PRISMA).

After a preliminary search of 159 papers, 16 studies were found to be highly relevant to the subject matter and were selected for full reviews, which yielded a broad range of themes and concepts consistent with the transition into full specialist status which can be categorized into the five main subject matters of Burnout, Identity, Relationships, Leadership and Unpreparedness in general competencies.

The results suggest that the transition involves broad psychological factors which impinge on the trainee in the pursuit of specialist stature and shows that it is almost never a smooth, simple process. As a scoping study though, this study only aims to present an overview of material reviewed and cannot determine whether studies provide robust findings. Further research examining wider publications is a priority.

list-item.p: Arksey H, O'Malley L. Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework. International journal of social research methodology. 2005;8:19-32
Teunissen PW, Westerman M. Opportunity or threat: the ambiguity of the consequences of transitions in medical education. Medical education. 2011;45:51-59

body.p: Presenting Author: Mohamed Ahmed
Supervisor: Dr Mary Higgins
Co-Supervisor: Dr Maria Farren

p.bold: THE TRANSITION TO MEDICAL STUDENT: A SCOPING REVIEW

body.p: Rae M1, Farren M2, Higgins M2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Perinatal Research Centre, University College Dublin, National Maternity Hospital, Dublin, Ireland

Transitions within the medical education continuum are periods of change in which students or doctors experience discontinuity which leads to the development of new behaviours1. Previous scoping reviews examining transitions within medical education mostly focused on transition from preclinical to clinical student and the transition from student to practitioner. The aim of this project was to complete a focused scoping review of the evidence on the transition to medical student.

Three databases, with focus on six specific medical education journals, were searched from 2010 to 2022 (PubMed, Embase and Scopus). The framework described by Arksey and O’Malley2 was followed over six stages. Research was guided by the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta Analyses extension for Scoping Review (PRISMA-ScR) tool. Twelve papers were selected for review.

Studies primarily looked at the experience of the transition to being a medical student or interventions to support the transition. The key themes that were identified were social support networks, self-efficacy, social cohesion and professional identity. Reported interventions included in person and online pre-matriculating programs, peer-mentor programs and structured extended orientation.

Each of the themes identified were a crucial factor in the transition to medical school. Many of the studies focused on a subgroup of medical students such as “non-traditional’ and “at risk students” rather than the transition experienced by medical students as a cohort. The studies were primarily conducted in North America; future studies in Europe could address this gap in the literature.

list-item.p: Westerman M, Teunissen PW. Transitions in medical education. In K Walsh (Ed.), Oxford textbook of medical education (pp. 372–381) Oxford, UK: Oxford University Press, 2013
Arksey H, O'Malley L. Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework. International journal of social research methodology. 2005;8(1):19-32.

body.p: Presenting Author:Mary Rae
Supervisor:Dr Mary Higgins
Co-Supervisor:Dr Maria Farren

p.bold: GP ACCESS TO DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING. A QUALITATIVE STUDY OF GPS’ EXPERIENCES

body.p: Gao J1, McCombe G1, Broughan J1, Fawsitt R,1,2, Collins C3, Feirteir M4, O’Callaghan M3,5, Quinlan D3, Stanley F3, Cullen W1
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Ireland East Hospital Group, Dublin, Ireland
3Irish College of General Practitioners, Dublin, Ireland
4Affidea Diagnostics, Ireland
5School of Medicine, University of Limerick, Limerick, Ireland

Diagnostic imaging is commonly used in medicine to confirm diagnosis and allow for medical intervention and treatment of certain illnesses and injuries. In March 2021 the HSE introduced the Enhanced GP Access to Diagnostics initiative to implement direct access by GP referral to x-ray, CT, MRI, DEXA and ultrasound for the full adult population of Ireland via private companies and hospitals (1). This provided 138,000 additional scans by the end of 2021. The aim of this study was to conduct semi-structured interviews to qualitatively explore Irish general practitioners’ experiences and insights of having enhanced direct access to diagnostic imaging.

Ten GPs participated in semi-structured telephone interviews during June and July 2022 to elicit their experiences and insights of having direct access to diagnostic imaging. Interviews were audio recorded, transcribed verbatim and checked for accuracy prior to analysis. Thematic analysis was carried out as informed by Braun and Clarke (2) using NVivo V.12 software.

Four key themes were identified: (1) patient care management; (2) practice workload; (3) hospital referrals; (4) management of the scheme. All GPs acknowledged having enhanced direct access to scans improved patients' care management and access to diagnostic services. However, there were concerns regarding increased workload for GPs and consensus that policy and funding supports would be required to counter the additional practice workload that results from diverting patients from ED/AMU.

The study highlights the significance of GP direct access to imaging services. The results can inform future policy and development of direct access to diagnostic imaging services in Irish general practice.
The author would like to acknowledge funding from the Health Research Board.

list-item.p: Health Service Executive. Winter Planning within the COVID-19 Pandemic. 2020.
Braun V, Clarke V. What can “thematic analysis” offer health and wellbeing researchers? . Taylor & Francis. 2014.

body.p: Presenting Author: Jiaran Gao
Supervisor: Dr Geoff McCombe
Co-Supervisor:Prof Walter Cullen

p.bold: LONG COVID IN GENERAL PRACTICE: EXPLORATORY MIXED-METHOD STUDY

body.p: Siu KYE1, Broughan J1, McCombe G1, Brennan J2, Collins C3, Fawsitt R4,5, Gallagher J1, Quinlan D4, Cullen W1
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Royal College of Physicians, Dublin, Ireland
3Irish College of General Practitioners, Dublin, Ireland
4Castle Gardens Surgery, Kilkenny, Ireland
5Ireland East Hospital Group, Dublin, Ireland

Long COVID is a novel condition with wide-ranging clinical presentation. This study aims to explore General Practitioners’ perceptions of long COVID care by assessing the feasibility and acceptability of data collection, exploring issues GPs encounter when delivering long COVID patient care, and what could better support them.

This exploratory mixed-methods study is informed by a pragmatic theoretical framework with the STROBE(1) and SRQR(2) guidelines. Fifteen eligible GPs from the Ireland East Network were invited by post with a questionnaire included. In-depth semi-structured interviews were arranged upon receipt of questionnaire responses.

Nine GPs completed questionnaires and interviews. Four GPs agreed, four neither agreed nor disagreed, and one disagreed with the statement ‘I am satisfied with the COVID-19-related care at my practice’. Regarding initiatives, eight GPs wanted closer links with secondary services, eight wanted therapeutic initiatives, six wanted diagnostic supports, five wanted more patient input, two wanted more remote care and one suggested other. Three themes were generated from the interviews – clinical presentation, diagnostic uncertainty, and continual care. Findings suggest a highly variable clinical presentation, with an urgent need for clinical guidelines, assessment tools, and a multidisciplinary and integrated approach to optimise patient care.

This research is novel as it focuses on long COVID within the Irish GP setting. Study shows procedures were feasible and acceptable. The findings suggest that long COVID may be debilitating, and GPs are well-placed to address patients’ needs. Further research is needed to better understand challenges regarding the assessment, diagnosis, and continuing care of long COVID patients.
The author would like to thank the Health Research Board, the Ireland East Hospital Group and the UCD College of Health and Agricultural Sciences and School of Medicine.

list-item.p: Von Elm E, Altman DG, Egger M, Pocock SJ, Gøtzsche PC, Vandenbroucke JP. The Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) statement: guidelines for reporting observational studies. Bulletin of the World Health Organization. 2007;85:867-72.
O’Brien BC, Harris IB, Beckman TJ, Reed DA, Cook DA. Standards for reporting qualitative research: a synthesis of recommendations. Academic medicine. 2014;89(9):1245-51.

body.p: Presenting Author: Ka Yuet Emily Siu
Supervisor: Prof Walter Cullen
Co-Supervisor: Mr John Broughan

p.bold: ENHANCED ACCESS TO DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING IN GENERAL PRACTICE: A SCOPING REVIEW OF PATIENT OUTCOMES

body.p: Phelan A1, Broughan J1, McCombe G1, Fawsitt R1,2, O’Callaghan M3, 4, Feirtear M5, Quinlan D4, Collins C4, Stanley F4, Cullen W1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Ireland East Hospital Group, Dublin, Ireland
3University of Limerick, Limerick, Ireland
4Irish College of General Practitioners, Ireland
5Affidea Diagnostics, Ireland

Direct access to diagnostic imaging in general practice provides an avenue to reduce referrals to consultant and to the emergency department services to ensure more timely diagnosis. Enhanced GP access to such services may potentially reduce hospital admissions, enhance patient care quality and improve disease outcomes [1]. This scoping review aims to gain a deeper understanding of how of direct access to diagnostic imaging in general practice impacts patient care and disease outcomes.

A search was conducted of ‘PubMed’, ‘Cochrane Library’, ‘Embase’ and ‘Google Scholar’ for papers published between 2012–2022 using Arksey and O’Malley’s framework for conducing scoping reviews [2]. The search process was guided by the ‘Preferred Reporting
Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses’ (PRISMA).

Twenty-three papers were included. The studies spanned numerous geographical locations (most commonly UK, Denmark, and The Netherlands), encompassing several study designs (most commonly cohort studies, randomised controlled trials and observational studies), and a range of populations and sample sizes. Among the impacts reported were reduced referrals to specialist and emergency departments, cost-effectiveness, and patient satisfaction.

Our findings indicate that enhanced direct access to diagnostic imaging in general practice can improve clinical outcomes in specific patient populations. Further research is warranted to explore the impact of multiple imaging modalities on whole populations. Appropriate and readily accessible radiology referral guidelines were identified as a priority concern. These guidelines should guide policy makers when planning and implementing future direct access initiatives, thus ensuring that access to diagnostic imaging is harmonised and equitable for the whole population.

list-item.p: O'Riordan M, Doran G, Collins C. Access to diagnostics in primary care and the impact on a primary care led health service. 2015.
Arksey H, O'Malley L. Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework. International journal of social research methodology. 2005;8(1):19-32.

body.p: Presenting Author: Amy Phelan
Supervisor: Prof Walter Cullen
Co-Supervisor: Mr John Broughan

p.bold: IMPROVING DETECTION AND MANAGEMENT OF FAMILIAL HYPERCHOLESTEROLAEMIA IN PRIMARY CARE: A SCOPING REVIEW

body.p: Khan AZ1, McCombe G1, McErlean S1, 2, Cullen W1, Brett T3, Gallagher J1, 2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2The Palms GP Surgery, Gorey, Wexford, Ireland
3University of Notre Dame, Australia

Familial Hypercholesterolaemia (FH) is a genetic condition characterised by a lifelong elevation of low density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-c). FH is one of the most common genetic diseases, with an estimated global prevalence of 1 in 250 individuals (1). However, it is both underdiagnosed and undertreated (1). Primary care can be a valuable asset for the opportunistic detection and management of FH. This review examines the strategies for improving the detection and management of FH in primary care.
Six electronic databases (PubMed, The Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, Web of
Science, CINAHL, ProQuest, and Scopus) were searched from May – June 2022 for articles published in English following Arksey and O’Malley’s six-stage scoping review process (2).

The initial search identified 1401 articles and a total of 30 studies were included in this review. A diverse range of methods have been studied for improving identification of FH. Three studies examined lipid outcomes from management in primary care. Two thirds of the studies with primary care management had a significant reduction in patient LDL-c levels.

The lack of consistency across the diagnostic criteria and the low number of studies addressing clinical outcomes are major features of this review. As a result, there is currently insufficient evidence to inform the most effective approach for the detection and management of FH in primary care.
Further research needs to be conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of the strategies for improving the detection and management of individuals with FH in primary care.

list-item.p: Wilemon KA, Patel J, Aguilar-Salinas C, Ahmed CD, Alkhnifsawi M, Almahmeed W, et al. Reducing the clinical and public health burden of familial hypercholesterolemia: a global call to action. JAMA cardiology. 2020;5(2):217-29.
Arksey H, O'Malley L. Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework. International journal of social research methodology. 2005;8(1):19-32.

body.p: Presenting Author: Abdullah Zafar Khan
Supervisor: Dr Joe Gallagher
Co-Supervisor: Dr Geoff McCombe

p.bold: CLINICAL EFFECTIVENESS OF GENERAL PRACTICE BASED SELF-MANAGEMENT SUPPORT AMONG PATIENTS AT HIGH RISK OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE: A SCOPING REVIEW

body.p: Grady E1, Broughan J1, McCombe G1, Morrissey J2, Treanor J2, Collins T2, Cullen W1
1School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland
2Irish Heart Foundation, Dublin, Ireland

Cardiovascular diseases (CVD) are the leading cause of mortality worldwide, yet many of the risk factors that contribute to this mortality are modifiable. General practice (GP) and primary care-based interventions have demonstrated promise in enabling patients to self-manage CVD risk. However, knowledge regarding the clinical effectiveness of these interventions is limited and this review aims to address the gap.

A six-stage scoping review framework developed by Arksey and O’Malley [1] with revisions by Levac et al [2], was used. The search process was guided by the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses – Extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR).

Thirty-four studies were included. The studies investigated GP or primary care-based interventions to reduce patients’ CVD risk and / or mitigate the impact of CVD risk factors (uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, and lifestyle factors such as diet, physical activity levels, smoking and alcohol consumption). Studies examined the efficacy and/or structure of the interventions. Clinical effectiveness was determined by improvement in systolic blood pressure, reduction in HbA1C levels and LDL cholesterol. Interventions involved nurse-led or lay personnel delivered initiatives, follow-up care, patient education, motivational interviewing and goal setting, medication adherence, accessibility of patient information leaflets, and access to additional supports such as smoking cessation or dietitian services.

Despite noted improvements in some of the studies, most of the results of these interventions thus far have not been statistically significant. Further large-scale and long-term studies are required to adequately assess the impact of these supports on high-risk patients.
The author would like to acknowledge funding from the family of the late Dr Mary J Farrell and the University College Dublin Ad Astra Academy.

list-item.p: Arksey, H. and L. O'Malley, Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework. International Journal of Social Research Methodology, 2005. 8(1): p. 19-32.
Levac, D., H. Colquhoun, and K.K. O'Brien, Scoping studies: advancing the methodology. Implementation Science, 2010. 5(1): p. 69.

body.p: Levac, D., H. Colquhoun, and K.K. O'Brien, Scoping studies: advancing the methodology. Implementation Science, 2010. 5(1): p. 69.
Presenting Author: Emma Grady
Supervisor: Mr John Broughan
Co-Supervisor: Prof Walter Cullen

p.bold: REDUCING READMISSIONS IN OLDER ADULTS THROUGH PRIMARY CARE INTERVENTIONS: A SCOPING REVIEW

body.p: Ossman J1, McCombe G1, Fawsitt R2, Doran P1, Queally M3, Lyons D2, Lynch T1, Merlin K4, Cullen W1
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Ireland East Hospital Group, Dublin, Ireland
3Health Services Executive, Ireland
4University of Puerto Rico, Puerto Rico

Hospital readmission is an important challenge for health services and is associated with increased healthcare costs and adverse health outcomes (1). Older adults are at high risk of readmission and targeting interventions to reduce readmissions is a priority. This review aims to examine primary care interventions designed to reduce readmissions among older adults.

The scoping review utilised Arksey and O’Malley’s six-stage framework (2). The search process was guided by the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA). Electronic database searches were performed on PubMed, the Cochrane Library and Google Scholar during June and July 2022. Thirty-four studies were considered relevant and included in the review. Interventions were grouped thematically based on the type of intervention used.

All the included studies involved an intervention aimed at older adults and patients with specific chronic diseases (most commonly congestive heart, COPD, and type II diabetes mellitus). A range of methodologies were used in the selected studies, most commonly randomized controlled trials. The most common interventions related to primary care physician visits either pre- or post-discharge, and either clinic or home based.

While the review did have some positive findings regarding post-discharge visits, there is no definitive evidence suggesting which interventions have the most beneficial effect on lowering readmission rates. Further research in this area is warranted.

list-item.p: Jencks SF, Williams MV, Coleman EA. Rehospitalizations among Patients in the Medicare Fee-for-Service Program. New England Journal of Medicine. 2009;360(14):1418-28.
Arksey H, O'Malley L. Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework. International journal of social research methodology. 2005;8(1):19-32.

body.p: Arksey H, O'Malley L. Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework. International journal of social research methodology. 2005;8(1):19-32.
Presenting Author: Janourine Ali Hassan Ossman
Supervisor: Dr Geoff McCombe
Co-Supervisor: Prof Walter Cullen

p.bold: EXPLORATION OF INTERCULTURAL COMPETENCE AMONG RADIOGRAPHY STUDENTS AND PRACTICE EDUCATORS

body.p: Lawati Z1, McGee A2, Davis M2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Radiography and Diagnostic Imaging, UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland

Ongoing changes in migration patterns have increased both national and ethnic diversities across populations. As a result, the concept of cultural competence has gained attention amongst all healthcare practitioners, including radiographers, to ensure quality healthcare delivery to diverse patient groups. Cultural competence in medicine is described as the understanding of the interaction between social and cultural factors with the health beliefs and behaviours of patients1. This study aimed to explore the attitudes and opinions of radiography students and practice educators regarding cultural competence relative to their professional role as radiographers.

Three focus group sessions were conducted with radiography practice educators (2 sessions) and undergraduate radiography students (1 session). These were held online via the Zoom platform. Data collected during participant discussions was analysed according to Braun and Clarke’s six phases of thematic analysis2.
Table 1 – Some of the initial codes generated during phase two of the data analysis
EthnicityBeliefs & normsAltering practiceModestyLanguage barriersConsentLiteracyWork environmentConfidence
Use of TechnologyLack of educationLack of experienceWorkflow impedimentReligionTrainingCommunicationBiasMinority groups
EmbracementHesitationCultures within the workplaceWorkshopsDiscussion formsReflective sessionsRole-play exercisesAuthorityTime

Seven individuals participated; 2 students and 5 practice educators. Thematic analysis identified variation among participants in their definition and experience of cultural competence relative to their professional role as radiographers. Issues such as obtaining consent and language barriers were themes frequently highlighted by participants, with the majority also acknowledging insufficient academic and clinical practice-based learning relative to managing cultural challenges in the healthcare setting.

Diversifying the student groups sent to clinical placement sites, weekly reflective sessions discussing the cultural challenges encountered and providing booklets containing frequently asked questions for radiography examinations in the most common languages spoken by presenting patients could increase students’ cultural competence and subsequently, the quality of healthcare provided.

list-item.p: Kaihlanen A-M, Hietapakka L, Heponiemi T., (2019). Increasing cultural awareness: Qualitative study of nurses’ perceptions about cultural competence training. BMC Nursing, 18, 38, pp1-9.
Braun, V. and Clarke, V., (2006). Using thematic analysis in psychology. Qualitative Research in Psychology, 3(2), pp.77-101.

body.p: Presenting Author: Zahra Al Lawati
Supervisor: Dr Allison McGee
Co-Supervisor: Dr Michaela Davis

p.bold: DECIPHERING DONOR- AND CELL-TYPE-SPECIFIC INTERACTIONS BETWEEN SCHIZOPHRENIA GENETIC RISK VARIANTS

body.p: Mennie KM1, Townsley KG2, Dobrindt K3, Brennand KJ3
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, New York, NY, USA
3Yale University School of Medicine, Yale University, New Haven, CT, USA

Schizophrenia (SCZ) is a polygenic neuropsychiatric and neurodevelopmental disorder [1]. The genetic liability for SCZ is largely due to non-coding, common variants which interact dynamically with genotype and environment [2]. Polygenic Risk Scores (PRS) estimate an individual’s genetic liability for a particular disease. Here, we investigate the transcriptomic differences between individuals with a high or low SCZ PRS but without a psychiatric diagnosis. In particular, we explore the effects of genetic background on gene expression in different brain cells – namely, astrocytes, GABAergic neurons, glutamatergic neurons, and neural progenitor cells – derived from human-induced pluripotent stem cells (hiPSCs).

We performed differential gene expression analysis comparing high versus low SCZ PRS donors for each cell type using Limma and EdgeR. We then conducted a meta-analysis (METAL) of the DGE results to identify changes in gene expression shared across cell types. To identify convergent biological pathways, we performed Gene Set Enrichment and Over Representation Analyses.

Few significantly differentially expressed genes (DEGs) overlapped between the different cell types while expression of DEGs was associated with distinct developmental periods. Our analyses suggest a particularly important role for GABAergic neurons during late prenatal brain development and middle adulthood. Shared gene expression converged on epigenetic and developmental pathways.

Here, the utility of hiPSC models in deciphering the donor- and cell-type-specific interactions between SCZ genetic risk variants is demonstrated. Because such interactions have important implications for susceptibility or resistance to SCZ, such studies pave the way to precision medicine and the identification of therapeutic interventions tailored to the individual.

list-item.p: Henriksen MG, Nordgaard J, Jansson LB. Genetics of Schizophrenia: Overview of Methods, Findings and Limitations. Front Hum Neurosci. 2017;11:322.
Rajarajan P, Flaherty E, Akbarian S, Brennand KJ. CRISPR-based functional evaluation of schizophrenia risk variants. Schizophr Res. 2020;217:26-36.

body.p: Presenting Author: Katrina Mennie
Supervisor: Dr Kristen Brennand

p.bold: SEXUALLY TRANSMITTED DISEASES IN IRELAND DURING THE 19

body.p: Tan J1, Cox C2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD School of History, Newman Building, University College Dublin, Ireland

Sexually transmitted diseases (STD) are a fascinating part of medical knowledge. With diseases such as the Human Papilloma Virus (HPV), Chlamydia, Gonorrhea and Syphilis known to the general population today. However medical knowledge was not as advanced during the 1800s to 1900s. This compounded with the fact that sexual education was not widely taught during that time led to a rise in STDs, known as Venereal Diseases, in that time. This study looks at both primary and secondary sources to better understand the climate of Ireland during this time. The study would then try to formulate a better understanding of venereal diseases, how the Irish public viewed them and ultimately what could be learnt from history.
Presenting Author: Joshua Tan Tai Kuang
Supervisor: Assoc Prof Catherine Cox

p.bold: PROFILING CIRCADIAN OSCILLATION IN PPARα mRNA EXPRESSION IN RAT RENAL PROXIMAL TUBULAR CELL CULTURE

body.p: Nino AG1, White J1,2, Docherty NG1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Diabetes Complications Research Centre, Conway Institute, University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland

Peroxisome proliferator activated receptor alpha (PPARα) serves as the target of the fibrate class of lipid-lowering drugs. PPARα expression and activity in the liver are influenced by the cellular clock [1] possibly reflecting its role in governing circadian patterns of energy metabolism. We aimed to characterize the circadian oscillation in Pparα expression in cultured rat renal tubular cells (NRK-52E) in order to identify a time window during which responses to fenofibrate treatment might be maximal.

NRK-52E cells were cultured to confluence, and according to an established method of synchronizing the cellular clock. Cells were treated for 2 hours with 100nm Dexamethasone then left to synchronize for 24 hours [2]. Ethanol vehicle treated cells served as a control. Cells were harvested at: 3, 11, 15 and 23 hours post synchronization, and cDNA was synthesized from RNA isolates. Then qPCR was performed using Pparα, Bmal1 and Per2 primers, the latter two are cellular clock genes, which show a reciprocal pattern of expression which can be assessed as evidence of the retention of circadian rhythms of gene expression in cultured cells. Two independent experiments were undertaken with internal replicates. The relative quantity of each gene was calculated using the ΔΔCt method using the 3-hour ethanol vehicle treated group as a calibrator.

In dexamethasone treated cells, the peak expression of Pparα was found at 3 and 23 hours post synchronization. However, this profile was also observed in vehicle treated cells. Moreover, dexamethasone appeared to ablate the normal reciprocal relationship of the Bmal1 and Per2 genes observed in vehicle treated cells.
The intrinsic Pparα oscillation implies that there may be a promising associated impact on the pharmacodynamic of fenofibrate treatment, a potentially renoprotective therapy in type 2 diabetes.
The author would like to acknowledge the opportunity, time and guidance provided by Assoc Prof Neil Docherty and James White.

list-item.p: Chen L, Yang G. PPARs Integrate the Mammalian Clock and Energy Metabolism. PPAR Research [Internet]. 2014 [cited 2022 Aug 6];2014:1–6. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3945976/

Gaspar LS, Álvaro AR, Carmo‐Silva S, Mendes AF, Relógio A, Cavadas C. The importance of determining circadian parameters in pharmacological studies. British Journal of Pharmacology [Internet]. 2019 Jul 6 [cited 2022 Jul 5];176(16):2827–47. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6637036/

body.p: Presenting Author: Abdallah G. Nino

p.bold: THE EFFECT OF ANTISENSE OLIGONUCLEOTIDE THERAPY IN A MOUSE MODEL OF HUNTINGTON’S DISEASE

body.p: Matthews M1, Tsanov M2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Conway Institute of Biomedical and Biomolecular Research, University College Dublin, Ireland

Huntington’s disease (HD) is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disease caused by CAG trinucleotide repeats in the huntingtin (HTT) gene on chromosome 4. A possible treatment for mutations in the HTT gene is anti-sense oligonucleotide therapy (ASO) which leads to a reduced amount of mutant HTT.

The aim of this research was to investigate the impact of ASO therapy on the navigational behaviour of experimental mice in a Barnes Maze. Three parameters assessed spatial learning: primary errors, hole deviation, and the angle of approach from initial position to the escape hole. Three groups of mice (ASO-treated, n=16; wild type, n=12; vehicle-treated, n=13) were compared over ten days of acquisition training. The data was analysed with a two-way ANOVA test, and groups were compared with Bonferroni’s multiple comparison test.

ASO treatment induced a non-significant trend in all three parameters. The ASO treated mice improved their average angle of approach from Day 1 (94°) to Day 10 (82°). Primary errors did not improve; hole deviation improved slightly from Day 1 (4.19) to Day 10 (3.44). However, the difference between the ASO and vehicle treated group was not statistically significant on any of the ten days of training.

This study suggests that ASO therapy’s improvement in navigational accuracy is not sufficient to induce therapeutic change. The use of Barnes maze to assess the behavioural improvement with ASO therapy may not be representative of a mouse’s improved cognitive abilities. Further studies with a different mouse model and longer period of assessment should be considered.

list-item.p: Southwell AL, Kordasiewicz HB, Langbehn D, Skotte NH, Parsons MP, Villanueva EB, et al. Huntingtin suppression restores cognitive function in a mouse model of Huntington’s disease. Science Translational Medicine [Internet]. 2018 Oct 3 [cited 2020 Apr 22];10(461). Available from: https://stm.sciencemag.org/content/10/461/eaar3959.abstract

body.p: Presenting Author: Miriam Matthews
Supervisor: Dr Marian Tsanov

p.bold: BIOMARKER DISCOVERY PREDICTING & MONITORING RESPONSE TO THERAPY

body.p: Hegarty N1, O’Neill A1, Dillon E2, Scaife C2, Cagney G2, Watson W1
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Conway Institute of Biomolecular & Biomedical Research, University College Dublin, Ireland

Treatment options for localized high-risk prostate cancer (PC) include surgery or radiation therapy, often optimized with neoadjuvant hormone therapy. Abiraterone acetate (AA) is an inhibitor of testosterone production. This study examines the addition of AA with GnRH agonist pre-radiation therapy. The primary objectives of this pilot study were to explore biomarker patterns to predict treatment response. The secondary objectives were to select the best biological matrix (serum or plasma) to detect these biomarkers and identify the most appropriate liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) approach to use.

We collected serum and plasma samples from 44 patients, with associated prostate specific antigen (PSA), testosterone & oestradiol levels, across five treatment timepoints. Post-treatment PSA below 0.1 ng/mL was deemed a successful response. We processed day zero plasma and serum samples for two patients (four total), using the iST proteomic sample preparation kit before analyzing with two LC-MS modalities, TimsTOF & QExactive.

Responders (n=32) showed lower initial Gleason scores (GS) versus non-responders (n=12), [GS 7/8 = 68.75% versus 41.66% respectively). The testosterone and oestradiol profiles were similar between groups. There was a similar number of proteins detected in serum and plasma (241 vs 239 respectively). The QExactive modality detected 228 proteins compared to TimsTOF’s 161 proteins.

Preliminary analysis has identified an association between GS and PSA response and that this response appears to be independent of hormone changes. QExactive represents the most sensitive LC-MS technology and neither sample type had a clear advantage for biomarker detection. We have identified potential proteins of interest for future studies.
Presenting Author: Nicholas Hegarty
Supervisor: Prof William Watson

p.bold: EQUITY ORIENTED PAIN MANAGEMENT FOR PEOPLE WHO USE DRUGS: A SCOPING REVIEW

body.p: Qiu LR1, Narayan S2, Maclure M3, McCracken R2, Klimas J2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Department of Family Practice, University of British Columbia, Vancouver BC, Canada
3Therapeutics Initiative, Department of Anesthesiology, Pharmacology and Therapeutics, University of British Columbia, Vancouver BC, Canada

Overdose health emergencies continue to drive drug-related mortality and morbidity. The recent changes in prescribing guidelines recommend prescribing fewer opioid analgesics and increasing non-opioid/non-pharmacological approaches. However, people who use drugs (PWUD) have been disproportionally affected by adverse events (opioid-related emergency department visits or hospitalizations) due to rapid reduction of opioids [1]. Furthermore, vulnerable groups, such as PWUD continue to experience barriers to accessing non-opioid pain management therapies. This scoping review identified the processes that limit access to the full spectrum of pain management therapies for PWUD.

We followed the Arksey and O’Malley scoping review method [2]. We generated search criteria for PubMed (MEDLINE) related to chronic pain, analgesics, abuse and equity-related terms. Our inclusion criteria included studies published since 2016 whose population was adult PWUD seeking pain management.

The electronic search identified 668 citations. After screening, 10 studies were included. Three major themes emerged from our scoping review 1) non-pharmacological treatments are effective, but barriers related to geography, physician knowledge, healthcare infrastructure, and most significantly cost exist. 2) The stigma of being a PWUD leads to poor patient-physician communication which led to denial of adequate prescription opioids 3) Suboptimal pain management led to patients being their own pain management specialists and resulted in increased illicit drug use, propagating a cycle of inadequate pain management.

This review identified several barriers that prevent PWUD from accessing a full range of pain management. PWUD, who experience pain in higher rates than the normal population, must receive equitable healthcare.
The author would like to acknowledge funding from the UCD SSRA International Dean’s Scholarship

list-item.p: Mark TL, Parish W. Opioid medication discontinuation and risk of adverse opioid-related health care events. Journal of substance abuse treatment. 2019 Aug 1;103:58-63.
Arksey H, O'Malley L. Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework. International journal of social research methodology. 2005 Feb 1;8(1):19-32.

body.p: Presenting Author:Lily Qiu
Supervisor:Dr Jano Klimas
Co-Supervisor:Dr Rita McCracken

p.bold: WHAT TREATMENT OPTIONS DO PATIENTS WITH OBESITY COMPLICATIONS PREFER?

body.p: Walley D1, Craig H2, Le Roux CW1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2Diabetes Complications Research Centre, UCD Conway Institute of Biomedical and Biomolecular Research, University College Dublin, Ireland

Obesity is recognised as a disease with more than 220 different complications including type 2 diabetes (T2D), chronic kidney disease (CKD) and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), all of which contributes to an increased in mortality. Patients presenting with obesity complications face challenges when deciding on treatment options for their disease. We aimed to understand the patients’ perspective of treatment options and what influences their decision.

We used photovoice, a form of participatory action research. We recruited six patients with obesity complications.2 Over two weeks, patients took photos representing their concerns of living with obesity complications, their hopes and desires from engaging in treatment. Interviews were then conducted with patients to discuss the images. We then completed a thematic analysis.

The overarching themes observed based on photovoice were self-blame about the participant’s weight compounded by a lack of support from health care professionals. Physical and emotional factors included a lack of mobility and anxiety impacting their intrapersonal environment. Treatment choice was a universal theme for all participants with the majority expressing concerns in respect to access, availability, cost and side effects. The majority preferred nutrition-based therapy with a minority choosing pharmacotherapy or surgical therapy.

Participants outlined their preferences for treatment and the factors which influence that process. The voice of patients with obesity complications needs to be considered when planning healthcare policy, guidelines, and services. Assessing factors that influence a patient’s choice about treatment will inform service providers and enable providers to address patient concerns and barriers to treatment.

list-item.p: Ansari S, Haboubi H, Haboubi N. Adult obesity complications: challenges and clinical impact. Ther Adv Endocrinol Metab. 2020,11:2042018820934955.
Creswell J. Educational Research: Planning, Conducting, and Evaluating. 4th ed. Boston: Pearson; 2012.

body.p: Presenting Author: David Walley
Supervisor:Prof Carel le Roux
Co-Supervisor:Ms Hilary Craig

p.bold: USING QUALITATIVE BEHAVIOURAL ASSESSMENT TO EVALUATE MOUSE BEHAVIOUR AS AN INDICATOR OF CHRONIC VERSUS ACUTE PAIN

body.p: Marin F1, 2, Martin M1, Mehigan S3, d’Alton M3, Reynolds AL2, 4
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD School of Veterinary Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
3UCD Biomedical Facility, University College Dublin, Ireland
4UCD Conway Institute of Biomedical and Biomolecular Research, University College Dublin, Ireland

The use of live animals in research is a controversial topic with the question of welfare at the forefront of discussions around the topic. There are many well-researched methods of measuring welfare in research animals, but many of them are invasive, require an extensive habituation period, or are only useful for measuring acute pain and not spontaneous/chronic pain. Qualitative behavioral analysis (QBA) is a relatively novel methodology being used to evaluate both acute and spontaneous/chronic pain in various species by evaluating the behaviours of the individuals to get an idea of their emotional and physical state. QBA is of particular interest as it is non-invasive, can be performed regardless of the research the animals undergo, and may allow observers to identify subtle expressions of pain a prey species might hide when observed via other methods. This project seeks to determine if QBA is a valid methodology for the assessment of chronic and acute pain in laboratory mice.

QBA was conducted retrospectively using pre-recorded videos of mice undergoing experiments. The two studies examined were seven weeks long and had ten and eleven mice in the experiments, respectively. Videos of the mice in their cages were taken twice a week (Monday and Friday) over 7 weeks, with an invasive procedure performed after the first recording. Nineteen fixed terms were used to attribute a score along a visual analogue scale for each mouse from minimum to maximum expression of the behaviors associated with each term to create a holistic picture of each individual animal.
A Shapiro-Wilk Test proved the data to be not normally distributed, identifying it as non-parametric.

An analysis was made of all 19 fixed terms across the timecourse of the experiment. Fixed terms associated with positive behavioural indicators such as “energetic”, “playful”, “determined”, “inquisitive”, “comfortable” and “content”, decreased over time, with a sharp decrease seen in weeks 6 and 7. Terms associated with negative behavioural indicators such as “agitated”, “tense”, “anxious” and “in pain” similarly increased towards the end of the experiment. Inter-assessor reliability testing will be performed.

Data suggests a difference in the behavioural expressions of mice at the start and end of the procedure which may highlight the ability of QBA testing to discriminate between acute and chronic painful states, however more testing must be performed.
Presenting Author: Fara Marin
Supervisor:Dr Mark d’Alton
Co-Supervisor:Dr Alison L Reynolds

p.bold: EFFECT OF SARS-CoV-2 VARIANT ON LONG COVID SYMPTOMOLOGY

body.p: Kalyansundaram K1, Chang G1, Dickson J1,2, Linnane S1,2
1UCD School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Beacon Hospital Academy, Dublin, Ireland

Long COVID is defined by the World Health Organisation as persistent symptoms that cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis following acute SARS-CoV-2 infection [1]. The pathophysiology is not understood with both biological and psychosocial aetiological mechanisms being proposed. We hypothesised that a biological process would result in different symptom profiles following acute infection with different SARS-CoV-2 variants.

Patients (n=353) referred to the Long COVID Clinic at the Beacon Hospital (47 males, 118 females, & 1 unspecified sex) completed symptom questionnaires. We reviewed variant data produced by the Health Protection Surveillance Centre [2] and defined Alpha variant infections occurring between 1/1/2021 and 31/5/2021 (n=79), Delta variant infections from 1/7/2021 to 30/11/2021 (n=62) and Omicron variant infections from 1/1/2022 onwards (n=25). Patient questionnaire data was analysed to identify differences in symptom prevalence according to variant infection.

Fatigue, breathlessness, sleep disturbance, anxiety & depression, and chest discomfort were the commonest symptoms across all variants. Myalgia, palpitations, brain fog, olfactory disturbance and headache affected a significant minority of patients. Fatigue, palpitations, brain fog and headache became less prevalent as the pandemic progressed while anxiety & depression, sleep disturbance, chest discomfort and olfactory disturbance increased. Breathlessness remained relatively consistent.

Long Covid triggered by different variants produces broadly similar symptoms. There are differences in the relative proportions of symptoms. This may indicate that differences in the acute infection trigger a different biological response and inform the search for a pathophysiological mechanism of Long Covid.

list-item.p: Long COVID: WHO issues clinical case definition [Internet]. www.medicalnewstoday.com. 2021 [cited 2022 Jun 7]. Available from: https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/who- issues-clinical-case-definition-of-long-covid#The-WHO-definition

Summary of COVID-19 virus variants in Ireland [Internet]. 2022 Jul [cited 2022 Aug 1]. Available from: https://www.hpsc.ie/az/respiratory/coronavirus/novelcoronavirus/surveillance/summaryofcovid- 19virusvariantsinireland/COVID_19_weekly_variant_report_website_20220626-final.pdf

body.p: Presenting Author:Kiranmala Kalyansundaram
Supervisor:Dr Seamus Linnane
Co-Supervisor:Ms Jane Dickson

p.bold: A COMPARISON OF CYATHOSTOMINS SPECIES POPULATION BETWEEN CLINICALLY NORMAL HORSES AND HORSE WITH ACUTE LARVAL CYATHOSTOMINOSIS USING MOLECULAR ANALYSIS

body.p: O’Neill F1, Gangotia D1, Zintl A1, Kiser L1, 2, Boxall O1, McSweeney D1, 2, Dunne S1, Lamb BR1, Walshe N1, Mulcahy G1, Byrne O1.
1UCD School of Veterinary Medicine, University College Dublin, Ireland
2UCD Conway Institute of Biomolecular & Biomedical Research, University College Dublin, Ireland

Cyathostomins are a parasitic nematode of the gastrointestinal tract impacting the equine population worldwide. There are over 40 species of cyathostomins, often occurring in mixed infections. Chronic and acute cyathostomins have disease implications, specifically acute larval cyathostominosis (ALC) has a poor prognosis. Conventionally, morphology methods was used to differentiate cyathostomin species. However, morphological identification is labor-intensive and requires expertise. Thus, molecular speciation can contribute to a better understanding of the species involved in the clinical disease. This study characterizes cyathostomin species through molecular speciation.

Cyathostomin worms were harvested from the caecum, right ventral colon, and left dorsal colon of 11 horses with no clinical signs and 1 horse that was euthanized due to ALC. Specifically, our work investigated worms from 2 clinically normal horses and the horse with ALC. DNA extraction from individual worms was performed. Following extraction, the ITS-2 region of worms was amplified and run on gel electrophoresis. Sequences were input into the Basic Alignment Search Tool for species determination.

We identified twelve cyathostomin species and one large strongyle species, Strongylus vulgaris. Interestingly, this study revealed that the non-clinical cases demonstrated a mixture of cyathostomins, whereas the horse suffering from ALC had only one species, Cylicocylus insigne.

Application of molecular speciation of cyathostomins is a valuable technique to understanding the possible contribution of species type to clinical disease. We provide a dataset that highlights the need for further investigation into the species-specific role in cyathostominosis and the varying pathogenicity of each species.
Presenting Author: Fiona O’Neill
Supervisor: Dr Nicola Walshe
Co-Supervisor: Prof Grace Mulcahy
